Informações institucionais
Em 1998 surge a NBS CEMA associação de duas tradicionais empresas de consultoria, que
atuavam em parceria desde 1995. As competências complementares da NBS em Gestão
Empresarial e as da CEMA em Meio Ambiente, vem permitindo um atendimento mais
abrangente e completo a seus clientes.
A NBS CEMA deste sua criação dedica-se a a integração de Sistemas de Gestão Ambiental
e de Segurança à Sistemas de Gestão da Qualidade, podendo inclusive:
- Gerenciar e Desenvolver com apoio do conhecimento de suas Controladoras NBS e
CEMA projetos complementares.
- Realizar Inventários de Gases de Efeito Estufa, Avaliações Ambiental e de Saúde e
Segurança Ocupacional iniciais, Auditorias de Sistemas, Treinamentos e Capacitação do
Pessoal e Projetos de sustentabilidade também estão no escopo do trabalho da NBS
CEMA.
Nestes anos de atuação, a NBS CEMA tem se destacado em diversos segmentos, com
clientes como Boticário, Syngenta, Volkswagen, GM, Yamana Gold, Eternit, Sama
Mineração, Ajinomoto, Vale, Itambé, Essencis, Estre, GlobalPack, DeBeers, Encalço
Engenharia etc.

4.4 Informações sobre contratos com clientes e fornecedores que incluam cláusulas vinculadas à elaboração de
inventários de GEE e/ou ao envio de informações relacionadas.
Não foi relatado.
4.5 Informações sobre incertezas, exclusões de fontes de dados e outras características da elaboração do
inventário.
Emissões fontes móveis, escopo 1:
Apesar dos veículos utilizados para as atividades comerciais e de consultoria sejam propriedade dos consultores, a
NBS CEMA entende que as emissões provenientes da combustão móvel são de escopo 1, pois:
a) Não são contabilizadas como escopo 1 pelo cliente;
b) A NBS CEMA tem controle sobre trajeto e horário das viagens;
c) As viagens comerciais e de consultoria não existiriam se não houvesse demanda controlada pela NBS CEMA;
d) As viagens comerciais e de consultoria foram consideradas como parte dos processos dos negócios da NBS
CEMA.
Emissões do Escopo 2:
O escritório da NBS-CEMA fica em salas comerciais, que são compartilhadas com mais duas empresas do grupo. As
contas de energia são divididas proporcionalmente entre as empresas. O cálculo, portanto foi feito considerando o
consumo de energia de salas comerciais, divididas proporcionalmente nas 3 empresas. Como exemplo, o consumo
médio das duas salas comerciais em 2013 foi é de 359 Kw/h mês. A parte que coube a NBS-CEMA, nesse período foi
de 19% do total. Se tirarmos 19% dos 359 KW/h chegamos ao valor aproximado médio para a NBS-CEMA é de
68KW/h. Mesmo no geral, o consumo médio de toda a organização é baixo, justamente por sermos uma empresa
de serviços e de consultoria e a maior parte de nossas atividades são desenvolvidas nas dependências dos clientes.
Vale lembrar que também consideramos, o consumo proporcional de energia elétrica do condomínio de escritórios
onde nos localizamos.
4.6 Descrição sobre ações internas para melhoria da qualidade do inventário de GEE. Por exemplo, sistematização
da coleta de dados, contratação de verificação externa, etc.

Declaração de Verificação de inventário de emissões de gases de efeito estufa
Programa Brasileiro GHG Protocol
Esta Declaração de Verificação1 documenta que o Organismo de Verificação (OV) citado abaixo realizou as
atividades de verificação de acordo com as Especificações de Verificação do Programa Brasileiro GHG Protocol
e a norma ABNT NBR ISO 14064-3:2007.
Todos os campos são de preenchimento obrigatório.
Organismo de Verificação (OV) Organização Inventariante (OI)
Nome do OV: BENRI Classificação da Produção de Nome da OI: NEOCERT CERTIFICAÇÕES AGRÍCOLAS
Açúcar e Etanol Ltda. FLORESTAIS E AGRÍCOLAS LTDA
Nome do verificador líder: Sergio Carvalho Nome do responsável pelo inventário: Barbara Rua
E-mail: srg.carv@terra.com.br E-mail: barbara@neocert.com.br
As emissões de gases de efeito estufa (GEE) informadas pela Organização Inventariante em seu inventário de
emissões, de 1º de janeiro até 31 de dezembro de 2023, são verificáveis e cumprem os requisitos do Programa
Brasileiro GHG Protocol, detalhados nas Especificações do Programa Brasileiro GHG Protocol de Contabilização,
Quantificação e Publicação de Inventários Corporativos de Emissões de Gases de Efeito Estufa (EPB).
Nível de Confiança
O Organismo de Verificação (OV) atribuiu o seguinte nível de confiança ao processo de verificação:
Verificação com nível de confiança razoável
“O inventário de gases de efeito estufa da organização inventariante para o ano de está materialmente
correto, é uma representação justa dos dados e informações de GEE e foi elaborado de acordo com as EPB.”
As limitações do processo de verificação foram:
Verificação com nível de confiança limitado
“Não há indícios de que o inventário de gases de efeito estufa da organização inventariante para o ano de
2023 não esteja materialmente correto, não seja uma representação justa dos dados e informações de GEE
e não tenha sido preparado de acordo com as EPB.”
As limitações do processo de verificação foram:
Inventário não verificável
Incluir razão, por exemplo: “devido a erros de dados” ou “não está de acordo com as EPB”:
Descrição do Escopo da Verificação
O inventário do ano de 2023 da organização inventariante foi verificado dentro do seguinte escopo:
Limites organizacionais Limites operacionais
Escopo 1
Controle operacional Escopo 2 – abordagem baseada em localização2
Participação societária Escopo 2 – abordagem baseada em escolha de compra2
Escopo 3

2 GENERAL
2.1 Project Description
2.1.1 Implementation Description
From the project starting date (01/05/2011) until December 2020, the project activities were
implemented in a total of 139.63 hectares. The first project activity instance (PAI#1) has a total of 79.5
hectares11 and includes the reforestation activities that happened from the project starting date until the end
of 2013, this first instance was monitored and verified in the first monitoring and crediting period in 2016
and it removed from the atmosphere 1543 t CO -e.
2
For this second monitoring period the PAI#1 reforestation areas had a total carbon stock of 4380 t
CO -e which represents an increase of 2837 tCO -e between 2016-2021.
2 2
Now in this second monitoring period (2021) a new project activity instance will be validate and verified,
the PAI#2 has a total of 60.07 hectares and encompass the reforestation activities that happened from
2016 to 2020, this second instance has removed 735 t CO -e in the 2021 monitoring period.
2
All the areas were characterized before the implementation of the project activities as degraded
pastures, abandoned and old crops, encompassing APP, private property, RL and common areas.
Since the project activities were implemented in abandoned areas, that did not give any economic
revenue to the landowner it had not occurred any leakage due the displacement of the activity to others
regions; also to guarantee the actual involvement of the landowner the OCT adopt a strategy to monitor the
leakage issue by giving an integrated technical assistance to all the property, in periodical visits to the
farms, showing and teaching the local producers and owners sustainable agricultural tools which enables
them to have a better production in less space, using in an efficient way the spatial boundaries of the
property, this was the solution to mitigate and avoid any possible leakage.
It is important to highlight that in this monitoring period has been included the nucleation reforestation
methodology in the GP activities. OCT has tested this model in some reforestation areas and had positive
results in terms of the landscape ecological restoration equal other models validated.
The project is not located under an FCPF Program12 or another jurisdiction with a jurisdictional
program13
11 The project area is 1.1 ha smaller than the previous monitoring period (80,6 hectares) because one of the agroforestry areas was
abandoned and converted to pasture by the landowner. Therefore, this area was removed from PAI#1 total area.
12 Brazil does not make part of Forest Carbon Partnership Facility, please refer to
https://www.forestcarbonpartnership.org/countries
13 The state of Bahia does not make part of any existing JNR.

MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
The OCT (Bahia Land Conservation Organization), based on environmental and socio-economical
sustainability principles, works together with public educational and research institutions to promote rural
communities’ autonomy inside the Pratigi Environmental Protection Area (EPA), with the goal of fomenting
a low carbon, income aggregating and biodiversity and environmental services conservation. The OCT
actions promote rural communities’ commitment with the integrated planning of properties, encouraging
and making worth the fundamental role of women and youth, linking work and income creation with the
conservation of natural and collectively used resources, important for agriculture. Aiming to diagnosis the
project impacts three phases were established to measure the interventions that happened during the first
monitoring period (2011-2016):
Phase I – The Participative Rural Appraisal (DRRP) aiming to set up the communities’ profiles and
established a social baseline (2012). The data has been used to evaluate and measure the community’s
involvement and the efficiency of the actions that had occurred, allowing quickly repairing of the route and
ensuring to reach of the expected results.
Phase II – The second meeting (2013) goal was to inform and validate the 2012 diagnosis results
together with the rural family farmers (local communities) and was evaluate the implementation process of
the actions and partial results of OCT’s projects.
Phase III – Result’s final evaluation meeting (2014) was measured the results of OCT’s projects
actions, to verify the real contributions to the improvement of the well-being in the communities located in
the Pratigi EPA.
These three phases were planned to be carried out along three years at the same communities at the
same time of the year to properly subsidize the evaluation process of the OCT’s results. The first two phases
became two documents: (i) DRRP to the base line establishment and (ii) Returning, to give back the results
of the first phase and evaluate the preliminary results of the projects carried out with the communities.
Nowadays, OCT, as the proponent of the Project, is using an innovative tool for the evaluation of the
progress and socio economical monitoring, which is the Barômetro da Sustentabilidade (Sustainability
Barometer). This is a methodology of sustainability evaluation developed by the IUCN and IDRC. The
methodology to the development of the BS is flexible, there is no fixed number of indicators and the choice
of the proper ones for each case is under the analyst’s responsibility, according to the possibility of
constructing Performance Measures to the study area and to the availability of information. The BS can be
used from the local to the global scale, allowing comparisons between different locals and through long
periods.
It is a systematic way to aggregate distinct indicators, which when isolated show just one aspect to
which they are related. The BS shows the local situation in relation to the sustainable development, allowing
the comparison of socio-economic conditions and of environmental biotic and abiotic characteristics.
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 14
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
Finally, regarding to the most significantly issues identified in the risk assessment, the project
proponent has signed a statement of commitment with the owners encompassed in the PAI#1 and PAI#2,
to avoid some risks raised in the risk assessment, ensuring the maintenance of restored areas and its
carbon stocks.
Biodiversity
In the first monitoring period the fauna biodiversity survey was conducted in partnership between State
University of Feira de Santana and OCT. A total of eight studies were developed by the university within
the Pratigi EPA boundaries: 1 study about ants, 3 studies regarding Euglossina bees, 1 study regarding
small-size, large-size and flying mammals and 3 studies about bird community.
Now, in the second monitoring period the project consolidates the fauna methodology, so the survey
was focused in the project areas and adjacent forest fragments, if the nearest fragments have an ecological
positive influence in the reforestation areas, especially in terms of species habitat growth, seed
dissemination and increase in the forest connectivity.
To determine the sampling areas of the terrestrial fauna of the Grouped Reforestation Project in the
Pratigi EPA, the environmental characteristics of the regions in question and ecological aspects of the
polygons belonging to the project were considered, such as, for example, polygon area, aggregate
distribution and size the area of the aggregated polygons, proximity to sources of biodiversity and distance
from homes and community centers and livestock production.
In this context, to better distribute and cover the sample area, five sample blocks were selected, two
blocks located north of the EPA, two blocks located more to the center, and one block located to the south.
For more information regarding the biodiversity sample design, please refer to section 5.3.2.
2.1.2 Project Category and Activity Type
The Project Activities Instances (PAI#1 and PAI#2) is part of the Reforestation Grouped Project at
Pratigi Environmental Protection Area.
It is registered under the VCS Program as sectoral scope 14 (AFOLU, Agriculture, Forestry and Other
Land Use) within the Afforestation, Reforestation and Revegetation (ARR) project category, since this
consists in increasing carbon sequestration by restoring native vegetative cover through seedling, sowing
and human-assisted natural regeneration.
Grouped project eligibility criteria
The PAI#1 and PAI#2 are the only activities instances currently under verification in the ambit of the
Pratigi EPA grouped project. It meets all the eligibility criteria, as follow:
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 15
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
Following VCS procedures, it has been analyzed all the areas part of the PAI#2 where the project
activities will be carried out was not a forest in 2006 (according to the definition below) and continued
without forest cover in 2016 when the project activity instance has started. The methodology to assess the
eligibility criteria of the project area to integrate this Grouped Project is described below:
For a determined area to be considered eligible, any pixel characterized as lacking vegetation from
the older date must also be lacking vegetation in the more recent image. To comply with the VCS protocol,
pixels are eligible if they are classified as deforested in images from 2006 and 2016.
The Brazilian Interministerial Commission on Global Climate Change, in its resolution no. 2, defines
forests as lands having growing trees with:
• A minimum tree crown cover of 30%.
• A minimum area of 1 hectare; and
• A minimum potential height of 5 meters at maturity.
These threshold values of the forest definition from the Brazilian governmental agency comply with
the UNFCCC definition and was used for this A/R project activity.
The criteria for including a Project Activity Instance (PAI) into the EPA Pratigi Grouped Project are:

2.1.7 Project Location (G3.3)
The validated reforestation grouped project encompass initially all the Pratigi EPA and a proposed
expansion area (see more details in the section 2.2.4), now the new GP boundary is expanding to a new
landscape approach in synergy with the definition of Ecological Corridors. OCT is expanding their activities
in other municipalities (Gandu, Nova Ibiá, Ubatã, Ibirataia and Barra do Rocha). The new GP boundaries
is considered one of the most biologically diverse areas in the Atlantic Forest Biome, with about
1,876,805ha spread over Southern Bahia (Figure 9).
After 10 years of project implementation, OCT is looking forward to scaling up the reforestation
activities, expanding all the expertise and know how acquired in the past to a landscape approach, through
the promotion of the GP activities to others local’s implementations institutions acting in the Atlantic Rain
Forest Central Corridor. So, through strategic partnerships will be possible to implement the ecologic
corridor main goal in one of the most biodiverse areas in Brazil, which is the landscape reforestation,
enabling a connection of the forest fragments, recovering the natural habitats through the physical
connection, and improving the genic flow in all the area.
Also, the new project proponent, BRC, has formalized the partnership with OCT to merge the
expertise’s from both institutions, the OCT with the field know how to restore the landscape and the BRC
support with all carbon demands of the project, including prospecting new investors and partners to
replicate the Pratigi Reforestation model to the rest of the Atlantic Rain Forest Central Corridor in the south
of Bahia State.
The GP has in this monitoring period two different project activity instances (PAI#1 and PAI#2), the
PAI#1 that started its first activities in 2011, was validated in 2014 and verified in 2016. The PAI#2 that is
seeking the validation (according to the GP rules and requirements) and verification, it encompasses all the
eligible reforestation areas from 2016-2020 with a total area of 60.07 hectares (Figure 9).

2.1.9 Other Programs (CL1.5)
Emissions Trading Programs and Other Binding Limits
Not applicable. The net GHG emission removals generated by the project will not be used for
compliance with any emissions trading program, neither to meet any kind of binding limits on GHG
emissions project. The project verified carbon units (VCU) will be traded and accounted via VCS registry
system (VERRA registry), so that information about buyer, sales or verification status may be traced
anytime by anyone, thus ensuring the guarantee of transparency, and protecting buyer from double
counting.
Other Forms of Environmental Credit
Not applicable. The project does not intend to generate any other form of GHG-related environmental
credits, beyond the VCUs, that will be registered in the VERRA Registry system.
Participation under Other GHG Programs
The Pratigi Reforestation Grouped Project was first validated under Climate, Community and
Biodiversity (CCB) Standard (Biodiversity Gold Level) in September 2013 and the validation statement was
issued under the VCS program on 28th October of 2014.
Also, the project has already been verified and the first verification statement was issued under the
VCS program on 27th November of 2017.
The project is only registered in the VERRA registry system under ID 1317.
2.1.10 Sustainable Development
The project will enable the mobilization and community awareness regarding to valuation and
restoration of environmental services and will also enable the beginning of the process of environmental
retrofitting of small properties (CAR - Rural Environmental Registry), stated in the national law 12.651/12.
In this regarding, the project activities are expected to become the forerunner of a new economy-focused
on the valuation of environmental assets of the Pratigi EPA.
The combination of recuperation of degraded lands, using of natural resource based on the
conservation, the agricultural production through agroforest systems and payment for environmental
services in small property, are perfectly in line with several sustainable development priorities and targets,
stated by the host country. This land use approach is supposed to create an economy model based on
conservation, providing to the small, medium, and large farms, options for income generation along with
biodiversity conservation.
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 31
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
The project is also in line with more specifically Brazilian’s strategies for sustainable development, as
the restoration, recovery, and low carbon agriculture to be implemented in 22 million hectares by 2030, as
announced in December 2016 at the 13th Conference of the Parties on Biological Diversity (COP13).
From these 22 million hectares, 12 million hectares are for the restoration and reforestation foreseen
in the Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDCs) and 10 million are foreseen in the Plan of Low
Carbon Agriculture (ABC Plan). Considering this, the grouped project will support the achievement of the
national sustainable development goals, both directly, by reforesting degraded areas, and indirectly, by
disseminating practical examples with the potential to be replicated, helping, above all, a paradigm shift in
the land use patterns.
As OCT is part of the PDCIS (see more details in the validated GP and in the section 4.1.2 of this
monitoring report). The PDCIS16 has their strategy of sustainable development based in 4 main groups
(human, productive, social, and environmental) which involves a great part of the 17 goals of the United
Nations, also this local development Program has earned in 2010 the recognition of the UN for these

sustainable production patterns in the farmers assisted by the organization. The main aspect of the
ATER activities in OCT is to promote a transition of the agriculture model in the region adopting

- Goal #13: Take urgent action to combat climate change and its impacts: OCT has as an institutional

restoration that directly contributes to the adaptation and mitigations of the climate change effects.

2.2.2 Methodology Deviations
One methodology deviation was adopted in the first monitoring period and is related with the use
of appendix 1 of AR-AM-tool-14, v4.2, in the measurement of fixed area plots in the first monitoring period
and actual monitoring period. Initially in the PD registered was defined only one allometric equation:
𝑌 = 21,297−6,953∗𝐷𝐴𝑃+0,74∗(𝐷𝐴𝑃2) Equation 1
Equation 1 - Allometric equation developed by TIEPOLO et al. (2002) used for the estimation of aboveground biomass
in Atlantic Forest for individuals with DBH greater than 4cm. Where Y = dry above-ground biomass (kg) and DAP =
diameter at breast height (1.3 m) in cm.
Thus, differently from what was stated in the GP monitoring plan, by the time of validation it was noted
during the first monitoring measurements that specially in the regeneration strata a considerable number
of samplings with diameter below of 4 cm.
According to the AR-AM-tool-14 and the GP, where the number of samplings with diameter below the
range of diameter applicable to the allometric equation is high, the mean biomass of the saplings in a
sample plot can be estimated as follows:
a) Determine the diameter mid-way between the diameter of the smallest sampling
existing and the smallest diameter allowed by the allometric equation.
b) Harvest from outside the plot area a few saplings having diameter close to the mid-
way diameter and obtain the mean biomass per sampling.
c) Count all the samplings in the sample plot and multiply this number by the mean
sapling biomass to obtain their contribution to the plot biomass.
However, taking consideration that the project objectives is to restore the landscape and the
environmental services related, removing the carbon from atmosphere. Is not economic and environmental
viable to the PP to harvest some samplings, especially in private areas of the landowners that are
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 34
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
participating in the project, this could contradict the PP mission and project objectives. Also, it´s necessary
to consider that a harvest approach is going to have some carbon emissions, and the generic instructions
in the tool can bring some statistical bias to the calculation.
So, the PP approach was to use the Saldarriaga et a. (1988) equation, a consolidate study with a
series of allometric equations, this new and more precise approach was chosen, as explained in section
4.2. This new equation was adopted to encompass trees between 1,27cm and 4cm DBH:
𝑌 = −0.292+0,369∗(𝐷𝐴𝑃2)−0,087∗𝐻
Equation 2
Equation 2 - Allometric equation of SALDARIAGA et al. (1988) used for the estimation of aboveground biomass for
individuals with DBH between 1,27cm and 4 cm. Where Y = dry biomass above ground (kg), DAP = diameter at breast
height (1.3 m) in cm and H = Height (m).
For this monitoring period (2016-2021) there is one new methodology deviation and is related to the
calculation of the number of sample plots, in the PD registered (section 4.3) it was defined that the PP was
going to use the AR-AM-Tool-03. The PP used the tool in the PAI#1 to calculate the number of sample
plots and the result was equal to 159 but the field measurements efforts were of 87 plots and in the PAI#2
the result was 99 sample plots, but the field efforts were 64 plots. According to the AR-AM-Tool-14, section
8.1.1, in the note part the tool suggests that the number of sample plots may be estimated using the Tool-
03, so is not mandatory the use of the tool.
The stratification procedure was carefully carried out to obtain homogeneous strata, however, due to
the substantial intra and interspecific variability within the project areas, it resulted in an associated
uncertainty exceeding 10%. To align with the Methodological Tool's requirements, we conservatively
adjusted the estimates by applying an uncertainty discount, as outlined in Appendix 2 of the AR-TOOL14
(Estimation of carbon stocks and changes in carbon stocks of trees and shrubs in A/R CDM project
activities). For additional information regarding the forest inventory and memory of calculus, please refer to
Section 3.2.4 and Annex II, respectively
2.2.3 Minor Changes to Project Description (Rules 3.5.6)
New biodiversity monitoring methodology
Following the observation (OBS# 04/17) made by the auditors in the first project verification, the fauna
biodiversity monitoring sampling was redesigned to specifically focus in the project areas.
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 35
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
During the first biodiversity monitoring period, OCT established a partnership with the State University
of Feira de Santana (UEFS), where several researchers and university graduate and post-graduate
students developed scientific works to evaluate the fauna from Pratigi EPA. The results obtained from these
studies were presented in the first monitoring report. The auditors evaluated these results and agreed with
the applied methodology, yet they raised an observation as an opportunity to improve the project
implementation and suggested that the monitoring activities performed in the project zone should be
periodically and specifically focused in the project areas. Therefore, the biodiversity monitoring design was
adjusted to improve the fauna monitoring.
The new design consists of five sample blocks that cover a total of 52 polygons (i.e., restored project
areas), which represents 25% of the total 222 polygons of the project. The number of polygons sampled
per block varied from 6 to 18 (Table 5). In addition to the reforestations inserted in the selected polygons,
the sampled environments also included the surroundings forest remnants and agroforestry systems. The
survey of surround environments is important since many species move through different habitats and
fragments.
Another small change concerns the fauna groups selected for monitoring. The PD predicted the
monitoring of birds and bees, mammals and ants were also included in the first monitoring. However, for
the second monitoring, the insects were excluded, and reptiles and amphibians were included. Known as
herpetofauna, the reptiles and amphibians were included because they are recognized as biological
indicators and can inform about the health status of both terrestrial and aquatic habitats. The amphibians
are specifically dependent on water resources, and since most of the project areas are riparian zones, the
inclusion of this group in fundamental to assess the impact of the project activities in biodiversity.
Therefore, the biodiversity impacts were measured through the assessment of four groups of
vertebrate fauna: birds, mammals, reptiles, and amphibians. In each sample block, a fauna group specialist
walked through irregular transects (with varying lengths), and actively searched for individuals. The specific
survey methodology for each group is described in detail in section 5.3 and Annex IX.

Project activities – new reforestation method, VCS PD section 1.8.
Nucleation or core areas with planting of native species is a forest restoration technique based on the
establishment of cores, measuring 4 m² consisting of five seedlings, deployed in a spacing of 10 x 10
meters. At each vertex, the cover species and in the center the diversity species, with spacing between 1.0
X 1.0-meter seedlings.
Figure 10. Nucleation technique planting scheme.
It is worth noting that the native species in the covering group are fast-growing, with the ecological
function of creating a favorable environment for the development of the species in the diversity group.
For the spatialization of the core areas, it’s recommended the use of the quincôncio method,
considering its efficiency for soil conservation. The beacon of the core areas starts from 3 meters of the
flooded area, demarcating the first line for this activity, it is used a rope with 24 meters marked in the center.
Thus, for an area of one hectare, 83 core areas will be implanted with the planting of 415 seedlings, in an
area of 332 m².
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 37
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
Figure 11. Representation of Quincôncio Method applied in the nucleation plantings.
In the formation of the nucleus, we carry out mowing (crowning) from the beacon of the seedling of
diversity within a radius of 2 meters, aiming to eliminate weed competition (Figure 12). For planting, 5 cribs
measuring 22 X 50 centimeters are opened, using the motor-digger equipment, and at this time, 150g of
super simple fertilizer is added, such as base fertilizer.
After the establishment of the core areas, the protocol of identification of individuals from regenerating
native species located between the core areas is applied. For this activity, the criterion of expression of
natural vegetation and height of individuals, considering the minimum height of 50 centimeters and the
maximum of one meter (Figure 13).
Figure 12. Mowing (crowning) from the beacon of the seedling within a radius of 2 meters, aiming to eliminate weed
competition.

Figure 13. Measuring of individuals height.
The restoration techniques applied in Bahia are still very incipient and punctual being generally led by
NGOs. Restoration actions, via core areas are examples of techniques that have not yet tested in the state
of Bahia. To validate this methodology, less costly than the total seedling, such as Core Areas/Nucleation
with “tree cores”, the Regeneration Conduction Natural and Passive Restoration, respectively, and thus
contribute to the development and consolidation of the Restoration Ecology, within the Southern Lowlands
and throughout the state of Bahia.
The study made by Federal University of Recôncavo Baiano (Annex VII) in partnership with OCT is
being conducted in Ibirapitanga within the boundaries of the Pratigi EPA. Three different methodologies are
being monitored and compared: passive restoration via natural regeneration, conduction of natural
regeneration and nucleation.
To monitor the progress of the regeneration the intersection of lines method was used. Change in
wealth and species composition in all springs. The increase in tree species in detriment to a dominant
composition of shrubs and herbs, in five of the nine springs sampled. Given the above, even with just one
year of implementation, and only two samples are notorious the positive effects. It is noteworthy that the
accumulation of data, the assessment of land cover, as well as the landscape metrics to be performed will
aid in a better interpretation of scaled data and the influence of the landscape, in triggering the natural
regeneration process.

for three important species (Hevea brasiliensis, Theobroma cacao and Cecropia sp) occurring in the SAF
strata. Thus, differently from what was stated in the GPD by the time of validation; the grouped project,

Grouped Project Expansion Boundaries – VCS PD section 1.9.
This Grouped Project initiated the reforestation action in the Pratigi EPA, located in Bahia state,
Northeastern Region in Brazil. The macro project zone deemed for this the grouped project is part of the
Atlantic Forest Central Corridor, which is considered one of the most biologically diverse areas in the
Atlantic Forest Biome, with about 12,280,000 ha 18spread over Southern Bahia, Espírito Santo, and a small
part of eastern Minas Gerais. For additional information regarding the physical parameters (e.g., soil,
geology, and climate), please refer to Annex XI.
In 2001, the Pratigi EPA area was expanded from its original area (49,000ha) to 85,686 ha. In 2010
OCT proposed to expand the EPA area to around 170,000 ha. This proposed area almost completely
covers the municipalities of Nilo Peçanha, Ituberá, Igrapiúna, Piraí do Norte and Ibirapitanga. The boundary
of the EPA is contained within the coordinates 13o35’S, 14o09’S, and 39o29’W, 38o57’W. Regardless of
the approval of the proposed expansion of the limit for Pratigi EPA, the Grouped Project includes the original
boundaries of the EPA and also the expansion area proposed by OCT, which is also the focus area of the
PDCIS. Thus, the GP boundary is that proposed by OCT and will be called herein as "Pratigi EPA".

The validated reforestation grouped project encompasses initially all the Pratigi EPA and a proposed
expansion area. The new GP boundary is going to expanding to a new landscape approach in synergy with
the definition of Ecological Corridors. Additionally the OCT is expanding their activities to other
municipalities (Gandu, Nova Ibiá, Ubatã, Ibirataia and Barra do Rocha). The new GP boundaries is
considered one of the most biologically diverse areas in the Atlantic Forest Biome, with about 1,876,805 ha
spread over Southern Bahia.
After 10 years of project implementation, OCT is looking forward to scaling up the reforestation
activities, expanding all the expertise and know how acquired in the past to a landscape approach, through
the promotion of the GP activities to others local’s implementations institutions acting in the Atlantic Rain
Forest Central Corridor. So, through strategic partnerships will be possible to implement the ecologic
corridor main goal in one of the most biodiverse areas in Brazil. The landscape reforestation, will enable a
connection of the forest fragments, recovering the natural habitats, through the physical connection,
improving the genic flow in all the area.
Also, the new project proponent, BRC, has formalized the partnership with OCT to merge the
expertise’s from both institutions, the OCT with the field know how to restore the landscape and the BRC
support with all carbon demands of the project, including prospecting new investors and partners to
replicate the Pratigi activities in other reforestation priorities areas in the Atlantic Rain Forest Central
Corridor.
Evaluation the expansion area in compliance with all small-scale AR project requirements.
To provide a comprehensive overview of the project's methodology and its impact assessment, a
detailed Planting Plan has been formulated. This plan encompasses an in-depth description of the land use
within the project boundaries, outlining the planting strategy, methodologies employed, management
approaches, restoration monitoring plan, and the scheduled expansion timeline. Furthermore, the
document includes ex-ante estimates aligned with the specified criteria, offering insights into the anticipated
removals and their compliance with the project's methodology.
Information obtained from the Rural Environmental Registration System (SICAR) reveal that most rural
properties in the project region are of small size, typically less than 20 hectares. This observation
underscores that the recently established boundaries are largely made up of smaller properties, each below
the 4 rural modules threshold. It is important to emphasize that the decision to include additional areas is
not solely determined by the size of the available space but is primarily influenced by factors such as
restoration prospects, financial resources, and management capabilities.
The project's extension to a new frontier in the southern microregion of Bahia encompasses a total
area of 1,876,805 hectares characterized by varied land use. Within this expansive region, diverse
landscapes unfold, featuring tropical forests, savannas, and mangroves. Notably, there are identified
pasture areas covering 309,520 hectares, which specifically offer substantial opportunities for development

2
developed or implemented by low-income communities and individuals as determined by the host Party”.
Please refer to file “small scale afforestation EB”, page 17 Considering that the CDM methodology
applicability and limits definition refer to the project activity instances, and not to the program of activities
(grouped project in the case of VCS), it means that to a new instance be eligible within the scope of the
small-scale methodology, that instance cannot generate more than 16 thousand VCUs/year, However as
stated in the above mentioned document, if a giving instance exceed this limit, it does not affect the eligibility
of this instance against the small scale methodology, it means that the only applicable restriction is that the
surplus tCERs (VCUs in the case of VCS) will not be eligible for issuance.

2.3.3 Conflicts and Grievances (G3.10)
There are no feedback and grievance redress during the second monitoring period. Considering the
current scope of the GPD, the procedure disclosure took place between 2016 to 2021. The internet is
operational, and communities can access OCT freely through its communication channel (commercial
WhatsApp account).
The consultation process follows the principles of requirement at the CCB standard and is structured
in three stages, each with reasonable time limits:
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 49

1. Initially, we attempt to resolve all complaints amicably and provide culturally appropriate written
responses.
2. Unresolved complaints from the first stage are then referred to mediation by a neutral third party.
3. If mediation does not lead to a resolution, the matter may be referred to arbitration (subject to local
laws) or the competent courts of the respective jurisdiction, with the option to involve a supranational
adjudicatory body, if applicable.
As explained in item 2.3.2, contacts, visits, conversations and feedback and complaints were always carried
out in person through an OCT technician, who listened to the owner's situation, shared it with the rest of
the team and delivered a solution. Generally, complaints and feedback were not related to the carbon
project, but rather about other needs for the area. There is no formal or written record of these complaints
or feedbacks, but these mechanisms are being created and will be presented in the next MRs
The OCT website is being redesigned for improvements. A specific page will be created for the Carbon
project, with a specific email and WhatsApp number: "Questions and Complaints Here."
So far, the feedback and grievance redress procedure has not been used by the communities.
Complaints filed will be systematized and a summary of the written responses will be presented at the next
meeting throughout the subsequent monitoring period.
All project documentation will be available on the project registration website (VERRA, Project ID 1317)
through the PD and monitoring reports to any stakeholder interested in additional project information.
a) Amicably negotiation:
• OCT will listen to grievances from communities and other relevant stakeholders, analyzed
them and provide a written response within one week via a commercial WhatsApp account
(+55 (73) 9812 0371), or
• OCT will listen to grievances and respond to them orally during public meetings, house
visits or general field work being held together with community representatives. In these
cases, the complaints and proposed resolutions will be registered by the OCT as
attachments to the monitoring reports.
b) Mediation by a neutral party: If a complaint cannot be amicably resolved through clarification
or redress, the dispute will be referred to mediation by an independent third party. This third
party may vary depending on the type of dispute. If there is a land dispute, the matter may be
referred to the state (CDA, SEMA) or federal (INCRA) land government agency ombudsman.
If there is a dispute over natural resources, the matter may be referred to the state (INEMA,
SEMA) or federal environmental agencies (IBAMA) ombudsmen’s or the ombudsman of those

2.3.4 Risks to the Project (G3.5)
In the Risk Analysis (Annex VIII) of the Monitoring Report present the main risks that can affect the
project benefits and the mitigation strategies undertaken by the project proponent and partners, during the
first and monitoring period of the PAI#1 and PAI#2.
The OCT, aiming at mitigating the project risks, especially those related to climate benefits and
biodiversity, during the project’s lifetime, identified the main risks, with a permanence risk assessment tool.
In this analysis it was possible to map, to identify and to propose strategic actions to mitigate each risk, as
is shown in Table 3.
The identified risks were the same that were identified in the non-permanence analysis of the project
and each one has a different strategy to be mitigate, both the risk and the mitigation were presented in table
3.
The social aspect has an additional tool the Sustainable Barometer (Barômetro da Sustentabilidade,
BS) to guarantee the success of the project activities. The pioneer use of this tool was possible because of
the results that OCT had used the Diagnóstico Rápido Rural Participativo (DRRP) which allowed the
identification the main risks to the community, mainly those with possible direct influence on the project
benefits.
The information and knowledge obtained during three years of diagnosis allowed the creation and
implementation of a monitoring routine of the communities, with a tool adapted by OCT to the Pratigi EPA,
called the Barômetro da Sustentabilidade (BS).
The methodology to the development of the BS is flexible, there is no fixed number of indicators and
the choice of the proper ones for each case is under the analyst’s responsibility, according to the possibility
of constructing Performance Measures to the study area and to the availability of information. The BS can
be used from the local to the global scale, allowing comparisons between different locals and time.
It is a systematic way to aggregate distinct indicators, which when isolated show just one aspect to
which they are related. The BS shows the local situation in relation to the sustainable development, allowing
the comparison of socio - economic conditions and of environmental biotic and abiotic characteristics. See
(Annex VI) with results of this tool in the monitoring period.
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 51
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
The financial viability was a risk, for example, identified in the table 3, it was mitigated due the fact that
the project proponent (OCT) has fundraising the reforestation activities from different donors and
investment funds with environmental results proposes, also part of the credits salles were guaranteed to
occur along the PAI#1 project lifetime. It’s important to highlight that the new project proponent (BRC) has
been helping to mitigate this risk aspects, especially because has used the revenues of the carbon credits
and markets to promote the reforestation activities.
Regarding to the opportunity costs the project was implemented, specially, in areas with legal status
as protected, such as Legal Reserve and Permanent Protected Areas when it was used the natural
regeneration and total seedling techniques. In the case of the Agroforestry technique, sometimes the
intervention happened in areas outside of protected areas and inside of the property, but this model permits
economic revenue, for example, in the first year (2013-2014) after the implementation had started to
mitigate the opportunity cost risk specially because of the return on some temporaries’ agricultural products
(pumpkin, beans, manioc, and other), in the second year (2014-2015) the economic return comes also from
the banana and in the third year (2016), with the first revenue from the cocoa nut comes to the landowner,
all the Opportunity costs were mitigated in the project.
Other risk that was identified during the validation process was from Land and Resource Tenures but
nowadays there is not any land conflict in the Pratigi EPA in the last 20 years. Approximately 60% of the
total area of Pratigi EPA are small producers and family farmers squatters of public lands (State Glebas)
but a local strategy is used to mitigate the risk, considerable, the community itself recognizes the land rights
and establishes boundaries between properties in an informal way, which is called land peaceful
possession. It´s important to quote that the project proponent has been in partnership with the State
Government to regularize the land in some locals of the EPA, also, besides, the CAR (Cadastro Ambiental
Rural) does not have any land legal status, it gives responsibility to the owner or to the possessor of the
natural resources inside a rural immobile.
And lastly, the natural risks have some aspects, but the major ones in the Pratigi EPA are:
a) From fire – the proponent encourages the landowners to use alternative methods to cleaning
the areas, such as mechanized cleaning, in the commitment term that OCT signed with the
local farm, there are a clause that is not allowed to the local producer to use fire any more in
our land. It worked efficiently even it had occurred a fire incident inside of a natural
regeneration area from FRVJ; this area was settled by the farm as a Legal Reserve, according
to some local neighbors it was occasioned by illegal hunter invasion that used the fire against
some animal, the area started to burn inside of the farm and had burned some parts outside
of the propriety.
b) From pest and disease outbreaks – In the areas of SAF implanted by OCT it was used the
cocoa plants resistant to the "Witches' broom" occurrence and the project proponent has been
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 52
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
giving technical assistance to management actions to mitigate any pest or disease in each
farm.
c) Competition between plants: Some areas of the PAI#1 (8,03 ha) submitted under the natural
regeneration model faced a strong competition with the pteridophytes, Pteridium sp., locally
known as “feto”. In this area some forest inventory plots presented zero t CO -e. In the areas
2
with high presence of “feto” OCT will implement in this year and in the next ones two strategies
in order to promote the natural regeneration: i) liming, once “feto” is favored by acid soils with
high rates of cation aluminum (Al+). ii) nucleation, where small “island” of trees is planted in
areas with low presence of trees or far from forest fragments, where the seeds bank is
insufficient for a regular natural regeneration
The overall risk rating (internal, external and natural risks) was evaluated in 3.5 but in the VCS
AFOLU permanence risk tool v4.0, section 2.5.2, besides the risk calculated before the minimum
risk rating shall be 10, so the buffer credits to be deposit in the AFOLU pooled buffer account will
represents 10% of the total emission removal obtained in this monitoring period in the first and
second project activity instances.
2.3.5 Enhancement of High Conservation Values (G3.6)
According to the registered CCB PD, in its section G.3.6:
“The project goal is the restoration of degraded areas for the maintenance and
enhancement of forest services, protecting natural resources and consequently the local
biodiversity. Thus, no high-value attribute for conservation will be adversely affected the project
activities. It will give, actually, at least the maintenance of such attributes, with likely improvement
of them”.
All High Conservation Values inside of the Grouped Project area were identified in the CCB PD, in its
section G.1.8. and the specific actions during the second monitoring period to maintenance or to enhance
the HCVs were:
a) The Pratigi EPA boundary is still the same from the validation period, thus the conservation
and protection paper from a protected area is still the same, which helps the protection of all
animal and plants exists in the area, despite of their importance or their level of endangerment
or endemism, so the HCVs due to this aspect had been kept.
b) In the aspect of the landscape, the project has been enhancing the connectivity between the
forest fragments, because all the reforested areas in PAI #1 and PAI#2 were choose
considering this ecological approach. Also, the project areas were focus on the reforestation
of Permanent Protection Areas (APPs) related with the hydric resources.
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 53
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
c) The Atlantic Rainforest is the Brazilian biome more degraded with the smaller forest remaining
and the project activities in the first years have been contributing to restore this important
biome and its related ecosystem, so enhance the HCVs in this aspect.
d) The reforestation activities due the focus to be implemented in hydric resources APPs is
enhance the HCV aspect in this indicator, because the vegetation contributes to prevent the
erosion and the rivers sedimentation and to help the hydric stability of the basins where had
the project activities because of the sponge effect of the forest.
e) The reforestation activities were implemented only in abandoned and degraded areas that
hadn´t any economic uses, and weren´t used as areas to supply the food demand of the local
communities, so this kind of HCV wasn´t impacted of the project activities.
f) And the last HCV from the use of natural resources by the community is not applicable to the
project, as it was mentioned in the CCB PD already validated.
2.3.6 Benefit Permanence (G3.7)
OCT took legal steps to ensure that the climate, community, and biodiversity benefits will be
maintained beyond the project lifetime. Each landowner parts of the PAI have a legal agreement (Annex V)
that was signed with the landowner to continue the management practices that will lead to the project area
reforestation for at least 30 years and guarantee the permanence of the carbon stocks in the post project
scenario and, as well, the community and biodiversity benefits:
Landowner responsibilities:
“(ii) Ensure the maintenance of areas and the legal protection necessary to ensure the
permanence of carbon stocks in the post-project scenario. In other words, to maintain the
integrity of the area object of the Carbon Credit Project, where no land use change of the
rural property is allowed;”
The focus area of the reforestation activities are Permanent Preservation Areas ("APP") and Legal
Reserve ("RL"), to promote all necessary acts for their protection and any guidelines that may eventually
be defined by the competent environmental agency, in the regarding the environmental regularization of
the property, in accordance with the standards established in the Project and the provisions of the term
celebrate with OCT.
The GP GHG, also, has an accounting period of 100 years. Any PAI lifetime can be extended until 100
years.

biodiversity).
b) Training related with the community's way of life (communities demand).
• Training and support for the management of solid waste in farms with a focus on
reduction and reuse. The process of waste separation, reuse and recycling on the
project areas will be addressed, assessing potential business opportunities.
• Promotion of environmental sanitation through the implantation of ecological toilets /
pits like: EMBRAPA pit, pit with banana and tire (evapotranspiration basin),
biodigester pit, dry toilet, etc.
• Environmental education training (e.g.: awareness of the importance of forests and
their different ecological functions; drinking water, environmental legislation, local
biodiversity).
• Capacity building / training related to Technical Assistance and Rural Extension
(ATER): ecological pasture; good practices for chestnuts and copaiba oil extraction,
good agricultural practices (subsistence agriculture), among others.
• Training focused on encouraging collective work in the community, promoting the local
associations.
Both strategies are disseminated to all community's member, with a gender inclusive approach,
focusing on the women engagement process, as well as on vulnerable or marginalized people.
However, the organization recognizes that more structured actions need to be adopted to target
vulnerable or marginalized groups to ensure equal job opportunity and specific goals need to be designed,
and are being planned to do so, in order to guarantee effective participation to this public in the project
activities. On this regarding, the hiring of 10 field technicians and 4 project managers is planned for next
year (2024), where affirmative policies to encourage the hiring of women, vulnerable or marginalized
people, will be adopted.
Also, it will be a character of periodical capacitation's to mitigate any possible staff turnover to avoid
any risk to the project implementation.
The selection process for hiring professionals needed for project activities was planned with the
aim of providing equal opportunities to all those interested in this type of work. Furthermore, the
opportunities were widely publicized through the communication tools traditionally used by OCT to
broadcast this type of advertisement, reaching the entire community."
For forest restoration work activities, the objective was set for specialized labor.

2.4.4 Relevant Laws and Regulations Related to Worker’s Rights (G4.5)
The Project proponent is committed to meet all applicable Brazilian laws and regulations applicable to
worker rights, Furthermore, have informed all workers about their rights, before hiring as its described in
the Brazilians Labors Laws.
The following is a list of Brazil’s relevant laws and regulations applicable to worker’s rights:
The Brazilian Constitution, Chapter II-Social Rights, Articles 7- 11 which addressed: I) Minimum wage,
ii) Normal working hours, iii) Guidance on vacation and weekly leave iv) Guidance on maternity and
paternity leave v) Recognition of collective bargaining vi) Prohibition of discrimination. In addition to the
Constitution, there are two additional decrees related to Brazilian labor laws.
Labor Federal Law (Consolidate of Working Laws (CLT): DECRETO-LEI N. º 5.452, DE 1º DE MAIO
DE 1943. This decree gives more clarification on the subjects: i) Hourly, daily, weekly, and monthly work
hours, ii) Employment of minors and women, iii) Establishes a minimum wage, iv) Worker safety and safe
working environments, v) Defines penalties for employer's non-compliance, vi) Establishes a judicial work-
related process for addressing all worker related issues. FEDERAL LAW Nº 5,889, June 8th of 1973
(Establishes Regular Norms for Rural Workers). This is a complementary law to the 1943 decree, once
prior to 1973, rural workers did not have the same rights as urban workers. In 1973, this law was established
to specify the equality between urban and rural workers, along with compensation for overtime.

2.4.5 Occupational Safety Assessment (G4.6)
OCT respect all safety rules and is currently in compliance with national labors laws that compel the
companies to promote and preserve employers’ health. Following the Brazilians Normative Rules OCT is
constantly improving the occupational health and safety management through the systematization of
actions for the identification, analysis, assessment, and control of occupational risk present in the
environments and workplaces where its employers work.
The risk analysis to the workers safety was made by grouping the job roles according to their activities
and risk exposure:
• Group 1 Office Activities - Administrative activities inherent to the positions, developed without
exposure to environmental risks.
• Group 2 Operational activities in an internal environment - Administrative/operational activities
inherent to the position developed without exposure to environmental risks.
• Group 3 Operational activities in the field - Activities developed in the field sporadically with the
possibility of using agents that pose a risk to health, with a low risk of exposure to the agents, under
normal working conditions and with the use of appropriate protection equipment. OCT promote the
use of individual protection equipment (boots, helmet, leggings, glasses and gloves) necessary to
the field activities to ensure the employee safety during work activities.
The complete work risks assessment is described in the Occupational Health Medical Control Program
(PCMSO - Programa de Controle Médico de Saúde Ocupacional) and the Environmental Risk Prevention
Program PPRA – Programa de Prevenção de Riscos Ambientais) available in Annex XIV.
For all activities at OCT, all groups of professionals are always informed of the risk relating to their
activities.The OCT team is trained and risks are discussed internally when planning activities. OCT also

2.5 Legal Status and Property Rights
2.5.1 National and Local Laws (G5.1)
Conceived in 1965, the Brazilian Forest Code19 was the first law regarding natural resources in Brazil.
It outlines the basic procedures for forest preservation and natural resource conservation in any forest areas
in Brazil. Though poorly enforced, the Brazilian Forest Code is extremely stringent and aims to preserve
and conserve the most important biodiversity spot in the globe, the Amazon Forest (MYERS et al., 2000).
The Code also protects the Atlantic Rainforest regions by defining areas considered Atlantic Rainforest
in Brazil and setting the amount (percentage) of areas to be preserved for land use and agricultural
activities, among others. The Pratigi Reforestation GP restored areas are defined by Federal law 12.651/12
as Permanent Preservation Area, as described below.
Permanent Preservation Area (APP-Área de Preservação Permanente)
APPs are protected areas where it is prohibited to cut down any vegetation unless for very specific
and limited reasons such as national security, public health, or public infrastructure. The law does not
require the owner to reforest the area in cases in which it is already degraded. It is worth mentioning that
National Forest Code is widespread not enforced.
Areas considered APP by the law, regardless of whether they are on public or private land, and that
are relevant to the project include:
• 30 meters on each side of water courses less than 10 m wide.
• 50 meters on each side of water courses between 10 and 50 m wide.
• 100 meters on each side of water courses between 50 and 200 m wide.
• 200 meters on each side of water courses between 200 and 600 m wide.
• 500 meters on each side of water courses greater than 600 m wide.
• Around ponds, lakes, water reservoirs, both natural and artificial.
• Around the springs and Water eye, whatever its topographical situation.
• Slopes, sandbanks, stabilizing dunes of mangroves, mangroves, edges.
• Top of hills, hills, mountains and hills, areas in altitude exceeding 1,800 meters, whatever
vegetation.
• Paths in the marginal range, in horizontal projection.
Also, the recent legislation proposed a cumulative approach for protection, as described in Table 6.

Monitoring Plan
The GP monitoring plan has followed the monitoring plan stated in the GP validated that was
elaborated to collect and handle all relevant data necessary for:
a) Guarantee that land subject to the project activity instance does not fall in wetland category.
b) Monitoring boundary of Project Activity Instance (PAI).
c) Monitoring of forest establishment to ensure the planting quality and alignment to the
project activities described in Section 1.8 of the registered GP and 2.2.4 of the present
monitoring report.
d) Verification of changes in carbon stocks in the pools selected.
e) Verification of project emissions and leakage emissions.
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 80
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
Besides the five items listed above, the monitoring plan also consider measures to guarantee that soil
disturbance attributable to the project activity does not cover more than 10 per cent of area22 in each of the
following types of land, when these lands are included within the project boundary:
a) Land containing organic soils.
b) Land which, in the baseline, is subjected to land-use and management practices and
receives inputs listed in appendices 1 and 2 the AR-AMS0007 v.3.1.
Regarding this, it is worth mentioning that the areas reforested under this Grouped Project do not
encompass areas with organic soil, once most of them are characterized as degraded pasture with low
percentage of organic matter (less than 12% of organic carbon in its first 20cm).
Also, the technology to be employed under the Grouped Project and its PAIs are based in four different
restoration models, as presented in section 2.1 of the MR. Only two of them had the potential to cause soil
disturbance: model 1 (Total Seedling) and model 3 (SAF), However, none of these two models, under the
PAI#1 and PAI#2, set aside cropland23 or grassland, but mainly in abandoned degraded pasture. Thus,
neither cropland nor grassland in the baseline was subjected to land-use and management practices such
described in appendices 2 and 3 of the referred methodology. However, if some PAI encompasses one of
these categories, measures (as manual plantation, and plow avoidance), will be adopted to avoid
disturbance in more than 10% of these areas.
The data collected was archived and will keep archived for a period of at least two years after the end
of the last crediting period of the project activity.
Guarantee that land subject to the project activity instance does not fall in wetland category.
As presented in section 2.2 of the validated GP, the wetlands were not considered in the GP boundary,
however as stated in the registered GP before any new PAI implementation the occurrence of this situation
should be checked to be avoided. In the case of PAI#1 and PAI#2, the field assessment was done,
confirming that there was no project activity carried out in wetland.
Monitoring boundary of Project Activity Instance (PAI)
The boundary of PAI#1 and #2 was defined in line with the Grouped Project area map. The location of
each polygon included in the PAI#1 and #2 was measured in the field using GPS. Results of these
measurements (georeferenced polygons) were consolidated in a unique PAI map and inserted into a
database and stored in electronic and paper form.
22 For example, digging pits of size 0.50 m × 0.50 m (length × width) at a spacing of 3 m × 3 m is equal
to a coverage of 2.78 per cent; continuous ploughing of land is equal to a coverage of 100 per cent.
23 Cropland does not make part of the Grouped project boundary

Monitoring of forest establishment to ensure the planting quality and alignment to the project
activities described in Section 1.8 of the registered GP.
Planting quality, planting method and survival was monitored by local foresters during and after

Verification of changes in carbon stocks in the pools selected24
i. Sampling design and stratification
Monitoring of Strata
Details of the initial stratification of the project area are presented in previous MR. However, as
predicted in the registered GP, the post stratification was done to address changes observed in the strata,
reducing intra-strata heterogeneity and sampling efforts, please refer to Annex I.
Calculation of the number of sample plots
The post stratification led to six strata in PAI#1 and PAI#2, where each one of them had a given
number of sample plots used to quantify the biomass changes during the first monitoring period. For more
information regarding sample efforts please refer to section 4.1, Annexes II, and I. For more information
regarding post strata definition please refer to Annex I. Following the standard procedure described by A/R
methodological tool 3 “Calculation of the number of sample plots for measurements within A/R CDM project
activities” v.2.1 used for crosschecking.

Sample plot size
Considering that both PAI#1 and PAI#2 are pulverized in several small areas, the sampling plot size
for the proposed reforestation project will be set at 100 m2 (10x10m). This plot size is according with the
fixed area method (REDEMAP, 2007) that guides the permanent plot installation in some aspect, such as
the geometry of the plot and the size.
Other biomass studies also use the same plot size, FIGUEIREIDO et al. (2000) for example, used in
his study in the forestry analysis of different kind of fragments. VOGEL et al. (2006) in his study to quantify
the forestry biomass in some dry forest in the south of Brazil, also used the same plot size adopted by the
Project Proponent in the GP. MEDEIROS AND AIDAR (2011) also used the same plot size in their study in
above ground biomass in an area of Atlantic Forest.
Determining plot location
Following the procedures of A/R tool 14 “Estimation of carbon stocks and change in carbon stocks of
trees and shrubs in A/R CDM project activities” v.4.2, the permanent sample plot location was defined using
the stratified random sampling, under this method random sample plots are installed in the strata and
measured. In the field the plots were found using two GPS. The geographical position (GPS coordinate),
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 83
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
administrative location, stratum and stand, series number of each plot were recorded in the field sheet and
physically (paper) and electronically archived.
The PP allocated the sampling plots proportionally in all the stratum to guarantee that 1% of the total
area of each stratum were measured. It was utilized the ArcGis 10 to random draw the points and thus the
centroids of the polygons of each permanent plot of 100m² (10x10m) in all stratums.
ii. Monitoring frequency
Although the verification and certification shall be carried out every five years after the first verification
until the end of the crediting period (paragraph 32 of decision 19/CP.9), the monitoring interval may be less
than five years. However, to reduce the monitoring cost, the monitoring intervals was conducted in a way
to coincide with verification time, i.e., five years of interval.
Thus, monitoring occurred at years 5, 10 (present document), 15, 20 and 25, after the start of the
project activities.
iii. Monitoring of the baseline net GHG removals by sinks.
According to AR-TOOL14 (Estimation of carbon stocks and change in carbon stocks of trees and
shrubs in A/R CDM project activities v.4.2)25 , the carbon stock in trees in the baseline can be accounted
as zero if all the following conditions are met:
a) The pre-project trees are neither harvested, nor cleared, nor removed throughout the
crediting period of the project activity.
b) The pre-project trees do not suffer mortality because of competition from trees planted in
the project, or damage because of implementation of the project activity, at any time during
the crediting period of the project activity.
c) The pre-project trees are not inventoried along with the project trees in monitoring of carbon
stocks but their continued existence, consistent with the baseline scenario, is monitored
throughout the crediting period of the project activity.
All the above-mentioned conditions are in line with the PAI#1 and PAI#2, as presented in section 2.2.
Furthermore, measures to guarantee the above-mentioned approaches was taken and was monitored by
the PP during the planting and the forest inventory, especially regarding to item (c) by the time of forest

inventory26. Based on this, the baseline carbon stock changes for PAI#1, 2 and next PAIs are deemed zero,
please refer to the registered GP to the baseline scenario.
iv. Monitoring of the actual net GHG removals by sinks
The monitoring plan described in this section was designed to meets both, AR-AMS0007 v.3.1 and
AR-TOOL14, monitoring requirements, as follow:
The monitoring of actual net GHG removals by sinks for each one of the PAIs registered under the
GP, will be based in the forest inventory procedures, according to the above paragraphs, as well as chapter
4 of the "Pacto pela restauração da mata atlântica: referencial dos conceitos e ações de restauração
florestal" (annex VII), what is in line with the requirement stated in paragraph 27 of AR-AMS0007 v.3.1:
"Information shall be provided, and recorded in the project design document, to establish that the commonly
accepted principles and practices of forest inventory and forest management in the host country are
implemented".
The monitoring framework of project GHG removals, therefore, was based in the application of
monitored parameters (listed in section 5.3) and non-monitored parameters (listed in section 5.2), to the
procedures and equations presented above in this section. In this regarding, the forest inventory, together
with the parameters available at validation, have provided the input data required by equations listed in the
section 3.2 below, enabling the PP to quantify and report the actual net GHG removals by the PAI#1 and
PAI#2, on this monitoring report. Please also refer to Annexes I and II.
Still according to the AR-AMS0007 v.3.1: "GHG emissions resulting from removal of herbaceous
vegetation, combustion of fossil fuel, fertilizer application, use of wood, decomposition of litter and fine roots
of N-fixing trees, construction of access roads within the project boundary, and transportation attributable
to the project activity shall be considered insignificant and therefore accounted as zero". Based on this and
considering that the use of fire does not make part of the project activities (please refer to registered GP,
section 2.2), emission due to project activities is expected to be zero for all PAI submitted under this GP.
v) Monitoring of project emissions and leakage
In ex-post estimates, the area burnt is measured according to NBR31 spectral index and verified with
high spatial resolution satellite imagery from Sentinel, along with ground verifications. Combustion and
emission factors are default values taken by IPCC (2006). Global warming potential is a default factor form
the latest IPCC assessment report.
26 During the forest inventory two parabotanicals with large empirical botanical knowledge keep up with the team in order to identify
the popular name of trees and determinate if the trees were introduced by the project activities or pre-existing. Whenever there was
some doubt on this regarding (pre or post project), the tree was not measured and considered pre project for conservativeness.
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 85
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
As described in section 4.3, no emission due to leakage is expected to occur under this GP,
notwithstanding leakage was monitored through periodically site visits and community monitoring events.
In case any field observations indicate a suspicion of leakage due to activity displacement from the
reforested area, assessment through satellite images27 will be carried out to identify and quantify the
extension of any eventual leakage. This last monitoring approach enabled the forest cover on the property
be verified accurately by the PP during the first monitoring period.
Based on the above-mentioned land use monitoring, no leakage due to project activities was identified,
thus no associated emission was necessary.
vi) Other elements of monitoring plan
Organizational structures and responsibilities
The project proponent (OCT) was in charge for the implementation and management of the monitoring
plan, while the other project proponent BRCARBON is responsible for the forest inventory activities (field
work and forest inventory report preparation – Annex I).
After the conclusion of the forest inventory and final report, BRC prepared the present monitoring
report, according to the monitoring plan and calculation framework described in section 3.2 of this
document.
The communities monitoring was done through the Sustainable Barometer (BS) and the baseline
scenario were constructed by the DRRP application. All the information regarding the community’s
assessment and monitoring can be seen in Annex VI and XII.
3.1.2 Dissemination of Monitoring Plan and Results (CL3.2)
The dissemination of the monitoring plan results (climate, biodiversity, and communities) to the
communities and stakeholders were in the OCT website, where before and after the verification process
the results were made publicly available on the internet.
Also, the OCT works as a rural extension institution, and promote meetings and interactions to the
communities direct and indirect impacted by the project activities, in this way the summaries and results,
related with the climate, biodiversity and communities were communicated to the communities and other
stakeholders along the visits and meetings in each community.
27 Satellite images will be used to generate land-use maps in the Grouped Project area, which will allow analysis of land-use
changes over time in the Grouped Project region.

Furthermore, measures to guarantee these approaches was taken and will monitored by the PP during
planting and forest inventory activities. Based on this, the baseline carbon stock changes for PAI#1 and
PAI#2, as well as others PAIs, is deemed zero, both to ex ante, as for the ex-post.
Still according to the AR-TOOL 14, the changes in carbon stocks in trees and shrubs in the baseline
may be accounted as zero for those lands for which the project participants can demonstrate, through
documentary evidence or through participatory rural appraisal (PRA), that the following indicators apply:
a) Observed reduction in topsoil depth (e.g., as shown by root exposure, presence of
pedestals, exposed sub-soil horizons);
b) Presence of gully, sheet, or rill erosion; or landslides, or other forms of mass-movement
erosion.
c) Presence of plant species locally known to be indicators of infertile land.
d) Land comprises of bare sand dunes, or other bare lands.

vegetation, combustion of fossil fuel, fertilizer application, use of wood, decomposition of litter and fine roots
of N-fixing trees, construction of access roads within the project boundary, and transportation attributable
to the project activity shall be considered insignificant and therefore accounted as zero.

After applying the last equations for the project activity, it was estimated the tree biomass per hectare
in a sample plot by using one of the plot measurement methods provided in table 1 of appendix 1 of AR
tool 14. Where the plot biomass values are estimated from direct or indirect measurements conducted on
trees in the sample plot. PP chosen to follow measurement of fixed area plots, which conducts both steps
below:
Step 1. Measurement (what is measured): individual tree dimension (e.g., diameter at breast height or
30cm from the ground, diameter at root collar, tree height, etc.);
Step 2. Conversion (how measurements are converted into tree biomass):
1. Using allometric32 equations based on the tree’s dimensions (DBH and H); or
2. Using biomass expansion factors; or
3. Combination of 1 and 2

3.2.3 Leakage
Leakage due to displacement of agricultural activities is unlikely because cattle removal was monitored
during monthly site visits of the PP (OCT) field professionals. Also, every five years, satellite images were
used to generate land-use maps in the Grouped Project area, which allowed a land-use changes analysis
over time in the Grouped Project region. Also, the forest cover on the properties with areas within the PAI#1
and PAI#2 has increased due the project activities.
Also, it’s important to highlight that the implementation of the project activities is focused in the
Permanent Preservation Areas and in the Legal Reserves, which, according to the Brazilian Law
(12651/2012), are considered as protected areas inside of the proprieties, so these areas are not allowed
to have any agricultural activities. The project activities are aligned to the Brazilian Law, especially
supporting the landowners in the environmental regularization of the proprieties.
For the PAI#1 and PAI#2 there were no people moving and as stated in the registered GP, all
proprieties included in the Grouped Project are supposed to participate spontaneously and voluntarily in a
mutual partnership between OCT and owners. Also, all the properties included in the PAI#1 and PAI#2
received a plan of property, where a detailed sketch of the properties is established, to encouraged to
introduce new production arrangements with the use of perennial crops with high added value, for example.
34 For the next monitoring periods of PAI#1 and PAI#2, if the contribution of carbon stock of shrubs seems to justify the additional
cost of the forest inventory, the ex-post calculation of this pool might be considered in the project removal. In this scenario, the
changes of shrubs carbon will use the “estimating carbon stock in shrubs at a point of time” (section 11 of the AR TOOL 14 v.4.1),
as stated in the registered GP and PAI#1 and 2.

During the periodically field monitoring it was not identified any cattle or crop activities displacement
from the PAI#1 and PAI#2 areas. This can be seen in the Field Registry from the Rural Assistance
performed by OCT, and in the CAR/CEFIR of the properties encompassed by the project activities. Based
on the assessment of the documents in addition to the field surveys and technical assistance visits, the
leakage emissions due to the PAI#1 and PAI#2 activities is zero for the second monitoring period, as
expected by the time of the project validation.
So, the leakage due to the displacement of agricultural activities in year t, as estimated in the tool

3.2.4 Net GHG Emission Reductions and Removals
This section summarizes the GHG emission removals for the PAI#1 and PAI#2 activities in the actual
monitoring period (2021).
As presented above in sections 3.2.1 and 3.2.3, the project, the baseline and the leakage emission
are deemed zero, thus the net change in carbon stocks due to the GP activities is equal to the gross GHG
emission removals, as presented in the Table 8. Although the stratification procedure was carefully carried
out to obtain homogeneous strata, the associated uncertainty of the carbon stock estimations was higher
than 10% (PAI#1 = 15% and PAI#2 = 27%). This happens due to the high heterogeneity intra and
interspecific existing among the native species used in the planting, and the heterogeneity in mortality rates
inter and intra plots of the same stratum. In these cases, the estimates are made conservative by applying
the uncertainty discount according to the procedure provided in Appendix 2 of the AR-TOOL14 (Estimation
of carbon stocks and change in carbon stocks of trees and shrubs in A/R CDM project activities). Therefore,
in order to follow the methodology procedure, we apply the discount factor according to the range of
uncertainty. For additional information regarding to the forest inventory and memory of calculus, please
refer to Annex II.
