3.3 Optional Criterion: Climate Change Adaptation Benefits
Restoring forest ecosystems works not only in the mitigation of climate changes through the GHG
removal by tree photosynthesis, but also improves the environmental and communities’ resilience against
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 101
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
the climatic extremes, as floods and droughts and regarding food security (please refer to SAF model) and
hydric security.
In this way, the AR projects based in SAFs and native species, promotes, in the ambit of climate changes
strategies, both, mitigation and adaptation.
The SAF model designed for the project is an agroforestry system, considered by the community
itself as if it were a "food company", through a mixture of crops, with plants that are always producing.
The benefits of implementing SAFs are that several sets of plants can be grown in the same area: rubber
tree, cocoa, cupuaçu, banana, açaí, jussara, cajá, papaya,
cassava, vegetables, native trees and other plants. These species were designed together with the
community and are used by them in their planting models.
Another benefit is that in an area where only cocoa is planted, more than 15 different types of plants
were considered. The calculations carried out in relation to the expense of the common practice of cocoa
cultivation were calculated, obtaining the same value, but with 2000 to 3000 thousand more productive and
edible plants in the same area, having a better use of land and greater variability of plants for consumption
or for sale.
Another benefit is that producers were able to turn an unproductive area into a productive area.
The plantations are useful to families, as they become a source of income and sustenance, called by
communities a "rural supermarket". Generating profit and preserving the environment at the same time.
SAF plantations bring a break in paradigms that were previously focused only on cocoa, they bring benefits
such as greater monetary value, they can last for many years, benefiting
the father, the children and the grandchildren.
The SAF changed the way communities work, it meant that the owner who has a SAF in his area
could work for himself, instead of working for others, providing autonomy.
From an adaptation point of view, SAFs are productive systems that are more resilient and adaptable
to climatic extremes, such as droughts and floods. This is because it has a greater variety of productive
species and the forest structure of these systems resemble the natural ecosystem, makes them more
resistant to droughts and floods. In addition to increasing family food security, they create a microclimate
around homes and favor the water resources of properties, improving the infiltration of water in the table,
the flow and water quality of rivers and springs.
.
The climate changes have direct impacts in the climate, communities, and biodiversity, as summarized
below:
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 102
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
• Climate: Increasing Forest vulnerability to fire, resulting in GHG emissions
• Community: water scarcity, restrictions on access to water, agricultural productivity
decrease and consequent restriction on food access, rural exodus.
• Biodiversity: droughts and desertification process, loss of biomass in the ecosystems and
direct loss of biodiversity.
Based in the above mentioned, the overall Grouped Project structure and implementation plan has a
general positive impact in the communities and the ecosystems, regarding adaptation to the climatic
changes.
Environment and biodiversity
The reforestation activity based on planting and assisting natural tree regeneration enhances the
environmental resilience against climate changes in several ways, as follow: Through soil conservation
promoting the microorganism’s biodiversity and fertility; promotes biodiversity through the restoration of
habitat and connecting forest fragments; preserve the water supplies (above-ground and below-ground)
and restore the Atlantic Rainforest native landscape.
All project activities have been designed for very limited or no negative environmental impact. There
is no burning or unnecessary tillage for soil preparation. All soil preparation in slope areas were conducted
manually without the use of any mechanical means. The most sensitive areas, such as riparian zones, did
not receive any chemical treatment either.
The main project climatic adaptation benefits, comes from the fortification of the natural environmental
features, as: reducing forest fragmentations, reducing soil erosion, and improving the watershed hydrologic
balance.
Forest fragmentation of the natural landscape affects the quality and quantity of the available habitat,
and consequently the survival of species, especially those endemic and threatened. Reforestation projects
contribute to forest habitat enlargement, to the buffering of forest remnants and to the improvement of forest
habitat connectivity; therefore, they are essential for enhancing the resiliency of biodiversity in the area.
The restoration and natural regeneration efforts have positive effects to reduce fauna loss and increase
fauna and flora colonization of the remainder’s areas of the Atlantic Rainforest.
Deforestation is also the main cause of soil degradation. In the Grouped Project area, deforestation
and forest degradation occurs because of cattle ranching and agricultural activities. There is a systematic
lack of sustainable management technics on this regarding.
The effect of the Grouped Project on the soils has been the improvement of soil stabilization in the
PAI#1 and PAI#2 areas. This occurs by increasing the root system (i.e., expanding the minerals fixation in
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 103
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
the sediment matrix), soil covering and promoting the organic matter deposition (e.g., litter). These elements
together have improved the soil physical, chemical and biological features.
In addition, the high infiltration rate in natural, mixed forests reduces the incidence of surface runoff
and erosion – especially in a context of climatic changes where floods are becoming more intense and
frequent. Also, the reduced soil water pressure and the binding effect of tree roots enhance slope stability,
which tends to reduce erosion (CALDER, 2002). Besides helping to stabilize stream banks, forests help to
reduce wastewater and chemical discharge into water bodies from upland areas and maintain cooler water
temperatures, thus improving dissolved oxygen levels in water (BROOKS et al., 1997). The deterioration of
the condition of the water and/or the structure of the water courses frequently leads to the loss of species
sensitive to pollution, the domination of tolerant species, and the general decrease in the taxonomic
richness or significant changes in the composition and functional roles of the community. In this regarding,
the project brings also positive effects on aquatic biodiversity.
The PPs understands that the restoration of native forest cover in the project basins is playing an
important role in the reconstitution of biological communities and the restoration of ecological processes
typical of upstream areas. In these areas, the smaller drainage basins are typically shaded by riparian
forests that, on the one hand restricts primary productivity, but on the other hand, constitutes the main
source of organic material and debris (e.g., leaf litter, branches, and riparian fruits) and of nutrients used
by biological communities. Finally, in these areas the riparian forests constitute the main foundation of
livelihood of an expressive diversity (and biomass) of macro invertebrates living in the benthic zone (various
organisms that live on, in, or near the substrate of bodies of water, such as aquatic insects, mollusks,
annelids and crustaceans, among others) and fish.
The restoration of riparian forest cover contributes not only to the conservation of river ecosystems
and their associated biodiversity, but also to the conservation of populations of animal species and
terrestrial plants through the increase in available forest habitat and of the connectivity between remaining
forests in the region and the restoration of key ecological processes in these environments. The role of
forest remnants and riparian forest corridors in maintaining biodiversity in the local landscape should be
considered essential, whether the surrounding context is predominantly pasture or eucalyptus monoculture.
Many studies have shown that, while areas of native regeneration and exotic tree plantations can provide
complementary conservation services, the value of primary forests remnants for biodiversity conservation
is irreplaceable (BARLOW et al., 2007). This is particularly true in a context of climatic changes, where
extreme weather is supposed to impose additional challengers for the wildlife survival.

4.1 Net Positive Community Impacts
4.1.1 Community Impacts (CM1.1)
As described in the project design, the boundaries of the grouped project involve the territory of the
Pratigi Environmental Protection Area, which covers five municipalities: Nilo Peçanha, Ituberá, Piraí do
Norte, Igrapiúna and Ibirapitanga, which are divided into Ecopolos: I Cordilheira, II Vale and III Estuário.
According to the last IBGE census (2010), these municipalities total a total of 84,900 inhabitants and
represent about 51% of the forest cover of the Lower South region of Bahia.
Since 2015, the Land Conservation Organization (OCT) has been accredited by the State Council
for Sustainable Rural Development of Bahia as an ATER service provider, thus expanding its capacity to
operate and provide qualified technical assistance, capable of leading farmers and their families to
participate in the permanent effort and improving productivity, while using practices and technologies that
conserve the environment. All activities developed by OCT are documented and published periodically in
the Annual Activity Report, which consists in a document that summarizes important data and facts arising
from the actions of the OCT, considering the scope and integrality of its operations. The reports published
during the second monitoring period are available in Annex X.
As a conservation strategy, OCT has implemented, over the years and in several projects,
Agroforestry Systems (SAF). Currently, it monitors the development of these through qualified technical
assistance. OCT and institutional partners monitor the carbon stocks of these production systems. Through
the integration of species agroforestry systems can increase the value of this as a carbon sequestration
system and consequently increases the removal, credits generation, providing diversity in the source of
revenue to farmers.
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 105
MONITORING REPORT:
CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
One direct impact to the communities by the reforestation is that the natural infrastructures are
important components in the management of hydrographic basins as they provide essential services, such
as control and filtration of sediments, improvement in water flow, mitigation of flood risk and revitalization
of rural areas. In water management, forest restoration as a strategy for implementing natural infrastructure
can provide services such as: a) erosion control, b) increase in soil permeability, c) mitigation of the effects
of floods, and d) regulation of water flow. These are services that support the conventional structures of
dams, reservoirs, retaining walls or water treatment plants (Filoso et al., 2017).
So, it can be considered that all proprieties that are part of the Reforestation Grouped Project has
experienced increased in the water quality. From the PAI#1 it is a total number of 36 proprieties if its
considered the average number of 3 peoples per family in Brazil (IBGE Database35) it´s possible to
considered that 108 persons had experienced increased in the water quality throughout the reforestation
activities in the first activity instance. In the PAI#2 the total number of proprieties is 84, so it's possible to
estimate that 252 persons had experienced increased in the water quality throughout the reforestation
activities in the second activity instance.

Currently, the data of the families and units served by OCT are stored in the virtual communication
and management platform developed in collaboration with "ManejeBem", where it is possible to obtain a
database about the registration of production and family units, the monitoring of technical assistance visits
and others. Manejebem is a technology-based that generates intelligence for the development of rural
family communities. It has solutions that help structure the agricultural production chain, through the
promotion of intelligence for decision-making in the field and the generation of profitable products with social
responsibility, being a pioneer in the collection of socio-agro-environmental data in a reliable, safe, and up-
to-date way of family farming.
One of the objectives of this platform is also to integrate the evaluation and monitoring of positive
impacts on the communities involved, in the dimensions that refer to the social, economic, and
environmental well-being of the production units, from the "Sustainability Barometer".
The barometer is a tool developed by Canadian experts linked to the institutions International Union
for Conservation of Nature – IUCN and International Development Research Center - IDRC for
sustainability assessment, whereas through a set of integrated indicators, it seeks to analyze the patterns
of interaction of people and the environment, through information about the quality of life and rate of
progress of a society towards sustainability (PRESCOTT ALLEN, 1999; VAN BELLEN, 2006).
As can be seen in the report of the last semester of 2020 (Annex X), even in the pandemic scenario
with access and face-to-face monitoring of technicians in the restricted areas, the capacity of care for
families assisted by OCT increased to 120 production units, which proves the ability to articulate and
mobilize the stakeholders of the territory.
4.1.2 Net Positive Community Well-Being Impacts (CM1.1)
During the second monitoring period of the project, OCT carried out several actions that positively
impacted the well-being not only for the community groups involved in the carbon project, but also of several
families of farmers, from the EPA Pratigi territory. The activities carried out, has benefited hundreds of
young people and their families, who could build a sustainable life in the rural zone, from knowledge and
new technologies aimed at the production of food with quality, safety, and access to fair markets, resulting
in returns worthy of their efforts. All activities developed by OCT are documented and published periodically
in the Annual Activity Report, which consists in a document that summarizes important data and facts
arising from the actions of the OCT, considering the scope and integrality of its operations. The reports
published during the second monitoring period are available in Annex X.
The Land Conservation Organization is part of the Integrated Development and Growth Program
with Sustainability (PDCIS), a social technology of proven impacts that strengthens family farming, this
program has expanded operations in 11 municipalities of the Mosaic of protected areas in the Lower South
of Bahia where about 285,000 people live. This initiative builds its results through participatory governance,
joining efforts with municipal, state, federal, private initiative, and civil society governments to promote the

productive development of families and simultaneously foster human, productive, social and environmental

It is important to remember that not all farmers who receive technical assistance carry out carbon
monitoring. Yet, OCT's main activities seek to promote environmental and productive conservation and
consolidate socio-economic and environmental development. To better systematize and measure its
results, in 2019 the OCT categorizes its activities into 12 main lines of action, which interact and converge
and are listed below:
• Production and sale of seedlings of species of the Atlantic Forest: OCT produces
seedlings of forest essentials of the Atlantic Forest for environmental and productive
conservation recovery actions.
• Conservation of remaining fragments of the Atlantic Forest: sum sums efforts for the
conservation and preservation of forests and springs.
• Landscape Planning: Addresses the integration of all actions in order to achieve a balanced
and sustainable environment.
• Integrated Property Plan (IPP): contains the land use and occupation map of the properties
and their suitability targeting the current productive panel and the future.
• Environmental Regularization: Environmental Legalization of the rural properties, through
its inclusion in the State Forest Registry of Rural Real Estate (CEFIR), corresponding in
Bahia to the Rural Environmental Registry. (CAR)
• Technical Assistance: enables farmers and their families to develop permanently and
improve productivity, to the fear that they use practices and technologies that preserve the
environment.
• Forest Restoration Actions: involves the recovery of degraded areas and the gradual
restoration of the forest, rescuing its biodiversity and ecological functions.
• Adequacy for participatory socio-environmental and organic certification recognizes
the application of good productive practices related to social, environmental and property
management issues
• Adequacy of rural sanitation: assisted families are oriented towards the proper disposal of
solid waste. In relation to liquids, were realized workshops of sanitation and installation of
economic septic tanks in the residences.
• Courses and workshops: contextualized education, focused on technical training of young
people between 18 and 29 years old. Between theory and practice, skilled young people
learn about property management and production techniques.
• Carbon Projects: The activities of the project promote the removal of greenhouse gases,
through the recovery of APP, primarily springs.

4.1.3 Protection of High Conservation Values (CM1.2)
The High Conservation Values (HCVs) related to community well-being are primarily represented by
HCV 4, which plays a pivotal role in preserving critical ecosystem services for local communities within this
project. The project is situated within the Pratigi Environmental Protection Area, a crucial part of the Central
Atlantic Forest Corridor. This area is of paramount importance for bird conservation and was officially
recognized by BirdLife International in 2012 as an integral component of the Endemic Bird Area (EBA) in
the southern region of Bahia, renowned for its remarkable bird endemism.
The attributes highlighted in the other HCVs associated with biodiversity significantly contribute to
sustaining HCV 4, given its central role in enhancing ecosystem services in the proximity of the project.
These forested areas are essential for providing critical environmental services, particularly in challenging
circumstances. Furthermore, it's essential to underscore that the project's primary focus is on riparian areas
for the maintenance of water resources. The restoration of these areas is instrumental in preventing erosion,
mitigating river sedimentation, and preserving the hydrological stability of the basins, thus amplifying the
benefits of HCV 4. It's noteworthy that the watersheds in this area are also designated as HCV 4 due to
their pivotal role in enhancing ecosystem services within the project zone.
The implementation of reforestation activities within the project is not expected to negatively impact
any HCVs associated with community well-being. To date, there have been no reports and/or evidence of
loss of quality or any harm related to the well-being of the community. However, if available information
suggest that management activities may pose a significant risk of causing severe or irreversible
environmental harm or threaten the well-being of the community, the project proponent will take explicit and
effective measures to prevent such harm and mitigate risks. This commitment aligns with the precautionary
principle and underscores the project's dedication to preserving the environment and safeguarding the
community's interests.
Among the high values identified by the project that are fundamental for the maintenance and
livelihoods of communities are the water resources and the protection of the region's watersheds, mainly
the Juliana Basin, one of the main reasons for creating the environmental protection area. The protection
of river basins will not be negatively impacted by the project group. In fact, the reforestation of degraded
areas, proposed by the project, has evident measurable positive impacts regarding the maintenance of
ecosystem services of provision and maintenance, such as the conservation of hydrographic basins and
soil protection, avoiding erosions and providing the maintenance of water quality.
One of the examples that demonstrates maintenance and conservation actions to the high
conservation values identified are the recovery actions of the APP and primarily springs of the region and
Pratigi, Ibirapitanga Water Producer program, where payment projects for environmental services are
contemplated. In addition to field visits for technical assistance in the project areas, OCT technicians also
periodically monitor the restored areas, using the rapid environmental diagnosis method. Where
environmental indicators are evaluated, such as number of springs, number of trees planted, density
CCB v2.0, VCS v3.0 112

CCB Version 2, VCS Version 3
(ind/ha), species richness, canopy coverage, height of individuals, isolation of areas, weed competition,
disturbances.

4.2 Offsite Stakeholder Impacts
4.2.1 Mitigation of Negative Impacts on Other Stakeholders (CM2.2)
It is not expected that negative social impacts will occur outside the Pratigi EPA due to the activities
of the grouped restoration project. As previously mentioned, reforestation activities is not supposed to
generate negative impacts on other stakeholders.
Notwithstanding, the project proponent operates not only on properties participating in the grouped
project, but also on neighboring properties, where the social indicators are monitored through the
application of the sustainability barometer during the technical and rural assistance visits not only to the
project's producers but throughout the OCT's region of influence. Whenever negative impacts on other
stakeholders or threats to community well-being be identified due to project activities, the project proponent
will take preventive and mitigating measures.

4.2.2 Net Impacts on Other Stakeholders (CM2.3)
Do not expect leaks to occur, nor relocations and interference in areas out of the project. For
conservative reasons, the OCT as a proponent remains monitoring all the landowners that might reallocate
the productive activities through a forested area. As, already said in this MR the ATER activities will avoid

5 BIODIVERSITY
5.1 Net Positive Biodiversity Impacts
5.1.1 Biodiversity Changes (B1.1)
When comparing the without-project and with-project scenarios, the validated CCB Project
Description predicted only positive impacts to biodiversity. The reforestation activities proposed on the
Grouped Project aim to promote connectivity between forest remains, enhance habitat heterogeneity,
preserve, and improve the conditions of local biodiversity.
5.1.2 High Conservation Value Protection (B1.2)
In the validated CCB PD, section G.1.8., the project area was classified as HCV1, HCV2, HCV3, and
HCV4. During the monitoring period, no HCV was adversely affected by the project activities, in fact, the
project has contributed to the maintenance and improvement of those attributes. The specific actions taken
during the second monitoring period to maintain or enhance the HCVs were:
• HCV1 (Globally, regionally, or nationally significant concentrations of biodiversity values) - The
Pratigi EPA boundary is still the same from the validation period, safeguarding the local fauna
and flora, despite their endangered level or endemism. Section 5.3.2 describes the
endangered and endemic species found within the project area, highlighting its importance to
protect biodiversity.
• HCV2 (Globally, regionally, or nationally significant large landscape level forests) – The project
activities enhanced the landscape aspect promoting connectivity between fragments through
restoration. All areas restored in PAI #1 were choose considering this ecological approach.
• HCV3 (Forest areas that are in or contain rare, threatened, or endangered ecosystems) – The
project is located in the Atlantic Rainforest, a biodiversity hotspot, with high level of endemism
and habitat loss, the project activities have been contributing to restore this important biome,
enhancing the HCV in this aspect.
• HCV4 (Forest areas that provide basic services of nature in critical situations) – The project
activities focused on Permanent Protection Areas of hydric resources, the restoration of these
areas contributes to prevent erosion, avoid river sedimentation, and support hydric stability of
the basins. These activities enhance HCV4.
5.1.3 Invasive Species (B1.3)
No invasive species was introduced by the project activities, neither have any invasive species
population increased as a result of project activities during the monitoring period. In the project areas
restored through total seedling planting were used only native species. Whereas in the Agroforestry
systems, three non-native species were used in the main model: cacao (Theobroma cacao), rubber-tree

5.2 Offsite Biodiversity Impacts
5.2.1 Negative Offsite Biodiversity Impact Mitigation (B2.2)
The positive benefits of forest restoration are well known, however, when not carefully planned or
executed, it can have negative impacts on local biodiversity. The project proponents are aware of all
potential negative effects that forest restoration can bring to both within and outside the restoration sites.
Therefore, the restoration procedure is designed to avoid all potential negative effects. The main recognized
risk that forest restoration can generate to local biodiversity is the introduction of invasive species. To avoid
this risk, OCT has made significant investments in establishing a seed supply chain that ensures integrity
of the seedlings. As part of these efforts, OCT has actively contributed to the formation of a Seed Collector
Network (Rede de Coletores de Sementes da APA do Pratigi) and the establishment of two thousand native
species trees matrices, serving as seed sources. These matrices are responsible for supplying the local
nursery, which has the capacity to produce more than 700 thousand seedlings annually.
Furthermore, OCT has forged a partnership with Recôncavo Baiano Federal University (UFRB) to
conduct scientific research within the restored areas, meticulously monitoring the success and efficacy of
the restoration methods. These multifaceted mechanisms ensure that the project activities will not introduce
invasive species and will not generate any adverse impact on the local biodiversity.
Therefore, the project did not have any negative impact on biodiversity, both in the project area and
outside its area of activity during the monitoring period.
5.2.2 Net Offsite Biodiversity Benefits (B2.3)
The project did not have any negative impact on biodiversity, both in the project area and outside its
area of activity. The forest restoration promoted by the project activities is improving the conditions of local
biodiversity by promoting connectivity between forest remains, enhancing habitat heterogeneity, improving
water quality and increasing ecosystem resilience. Thus, since the project has no negative impacts
identified, the project net effect on biodiversity is positive.

5.3.2 Biodiversity Monitoring Results (B3.1, B3.2)
The biodiversity impacts were measured through the assessment of wood tree species and five groups
of vertebrate fauna: birds, mammals, reptiles, and amphibians.
The wood tree species diversity data was obtained from the carbon forest inventory. Whereas to obtain
the fauna biodiversity data, the project area was divided into five sample blocks (two blocks in PAI#1 and
three blocks in PAI#2), which comprised 25% of the total 222 project polygons (Figure 28). In each block,
a fauna group specialist walked through irregular transects (with varying lengths), and actively searched for
individuals. All encountered individuals were identified at the nearest taxonomic level and classified
according to their threat conservation status. The conservation status of the bird species was evaluated
according to IUCN Red List of Threatened Species (IUCN, 2021) and two national classifications, one at
the state level (SEMA, 2017) and another at the federal level (ICMBIO, 2018).
The fauna monitoring was conducted between August 12th and 24th, 2021. The summary results,
divided by groups, are presented below, the complete monitoring report is available in Annex IX.

Fauna
Birds
The bird species surveys were conducted in two different periods: from sunrise until 1 pm and from 3
pm until sunset, totalizing 12 hours of survey per day. The surveys were carried out for 13 days, resulting
in 156 hours of sampling effort. All visual and/or auditory records that resulted in undoubted species
identification were considered. The identification process was conducted with the help of binoculars
(Bushnell 10x42), recorders (Sony PX240 e Roland R05) and identification guides.
A total of 229 bird species belonging to 49 families and 19 ecological orders were recorded. This
richness corresponds to 57,8% of birds that may occur in the Bahia state Atlantic Forest. The Passeriformes
was the most common order, representing 61% of total registered avifauna. The most common families
were Thraupidae, Tyrannidae, and Trochilidae with, 25, 23, and 19 species, respectively (Figure 29). The
Thraupidae family is composed by species such as saíras (Tangaras cayana, Tangara cyanomelas) (Figure
31) and canários (Sicalis flaveola) (Figure 32).

Mammals
Due to the wide range of habitats used by medium and large mammals, it was necessary to apply
direct and indirect survey methods, such as active search through irregular transects, identification of traces
(trails, feces, burrows, and others) and camera traps (CULLEN et al., 2003; TOMAS and MIRANDA, 2003).
The active searches were performed twice a day, from sunrise until 1 pm and from 3 pm until twilight/night
period. The surveys were conducted through 12 days, totalizing 144 hours of sampling effort for this methos.
The transects were carried out respecting an average speed of 1km/h. The records were noted in a field
spreadsheet and, when possible, confirmed through photograph (Erro! Fonte de referência não
encontrada.).

Ten camera traps were installed during the mammal’s survey, two cameras in each sample block,
recording 24 hours per day, through 12 days, totalizing 2880 hour of sampling for this method. To enhance
the records, attractive baits were used.
A total of 22 species of mammals, belonging to 6 order and 15 families were recorded, of which, five
(Callicebus melanochir, Guerlinguetus brasiliensis, Callithrix kuhlii, Didelphis aurita e Sapajus
xanthosternos) are endemic to Atlantic Rainforest. The survey methodology focused on medium and large
size mammals (body weight higher than 1 kg), however, one small size mammal, Guerlinguetus brasiliensis
(esquilo), was occasionally recorded and included on the final species occurrence list.
The Carnivora order was the most representative, with seven species belonging to four distinct
families: Canidae (Cerdocyon thous), Felidae (Leopardus guttulus e Puma concolor), Mustelidae (Eira
barbara e Lontra longicaudis) and Procyonidae (Potos flavus e Procyon cancrivorus). Species of this order
have low population densities and are extremely vulnerable, especially the mustelid Lontra longicaudis
(lontra) and the felids Leopardus guttulus (gato-do-mato) and Puma concolor (onça-parda) classified as
vulnerable and/or endangered by national and international endangered species lists (Table ). The main
threats to this species are habitat loss and fragmentation caused by urban and agriculture expansions, fire,
hunting (Figure 34), retaliatory killing (human response to fauna preying on livestock) and accidental road
crashes (AZEVEDO et al., 2013; RODRIGUES, 2013; OLIVEIRA et al., 2016, PETERS et al., 2016).
Three primate species (Sapajus xanthosternos, Callicebus melanochir and Callithrix kuhlii) (Table )
are also classified under some threat level by national and international endangered species lists. Its
populations are consistently decreasing over the years due to habitat loss, fragmentation, and
domestication (CANALE et al., 2015; PEREIRA, 2015; PRINTES AND JERUSALINSKY, 2015).

Herpetofauna
The herpetofauna survey was conducted through a time-limited active search, two times a day, in the
morning (8 am - 12 pm) and twilight/night (6 pm - 10 pm). The active search methodology aims to collect
as many species as possible, therefore, the group specialist searched in micro-habitats where amphibians
and reptiles may be active or sheltered, such as leaf litter, fallen tree trunks, under rocks, ground holes,
termite mounds, tree barks, bromeliads, temporary ponds, floodplain areas, streams, and others (Figure
35) (CAMPBELL AND CHRISTMAM, 1982; MARTINS AND OLIVEIRA, 1998; DUELLMAN AND TRUEB,
1986). Any species encountered by chance while the team was moving from one area to another was also
registered. The survey was conducted for 13 days, totalizing 130 hours of sampling.
A total of 30 amphibian species, comprising 5 families and 12 genera were found within the project
area. All species belong to the Anura order (i.e., toads, frogs, and tree frogs). Thirteen species are endemic
to Atlantic Rainforest and one, Bokermannohyla capra, is endemic to Bahia state.
Most recorded species are classified under the Least Concern (LC) category of threat, this is a low-
risk category applied to species with abundant populations and wide distribution range. Regarding the
habitat preferences, almost half (46.67%) of the species recorded (n=14) are from forest areas, 30% (n=9)
are from open areas and 16.67% (n=5) are from both areas. Two species were not classified according to
their habitat preferences. In general, all amphibian species depend directly or indirectly on forest
environments since these habitats provide shelter and food resources for adults and young individuals
(SILVA AND ROSSA-FERES, 2007). Therefore, reforestation activities, especially those performed to
protect water bodies, are fundamental to amphibian conservation.
For the reptiles, the survey found a total of 10 species, of which, four were snakes and 6 lizards. Only
one specie recorded is endemic to the Atlantic Forest, the lizard Enyalius catenatus (papa-vento).
Regarding the habitat preferences, 50% (n=5) are from both forest and open areas, 30% (n=3) are from
forest areas, and 20% (n=2) are from open areas. No registered reptiles are classified as endangered.

5.3.3 Monitoring Plan and Results Dissemination (B3.3)
As described in the first monitoring report, the dissemination of the monitoring plan results (climate,
biodiversity, and communities) to the communities and stakeholders were made through the OCT website.
Moreover, OCT works as a rural extension institution, and hold several meeting and interactions with the
communities that were directly or indirectly impacted by the project activities. In these meetings, the
summaries and results related to climate, community, and biodiversity were communicated to the
communities and other stakeholders.

Summary
RINA Services S.p.A. (RINA), commissioned by BRCarbon Serviços Ambientais LTDA, has verified the
monitoring report and greenhouse gas emission reductions reported for the project activity Brazilian
Amazon APD Grouped Project in Brazil, with regards to relevant requirements for VCS rules and against
all criteria set out by the CCBA in its Climate, Community & Biodiversity Standards: v3.1. The objective
of the Brazilian Amazon APD Grouped Project is to protect Amazon rainforest of 27,626.34 ha in 9 project
activity instances (PAI). The area of this properties is distributed in municipalities of the Brazilian Legal
Amazon: Aripuanã in Mato Grosso state (PAI#1), Sena Madureira and Rio Branco in Acre state (PAIs#2-
5), Manicoré in Amazon state (PAI#6) and Rondon do Pará, Tomé-Açu and Ipixuna do Pará in Pará state
(PAIs#7-9).
This project utilizes the approved VCS Methodology VM0007: REDD+ Methodology Framework (REDD+
MF) version 1.6 of 08/09/2020, and corresponding modules/ tools are applied to quantify the GHG
removals achieved in this project.
The BRCarbon Serviços Ambientais LTDA has requested RINA to perform the VCS and CCB verification
for which a CCB & VCS Monitoring Report, a Non-Permanence-Risk Report and supporting documents
were provided. RINA, acting as an independent third party, has assessed the documents and evidence
provided, and performed desk assessment and virtual and presential assessment, which included a desk
review, inspection of project site, interviews with stakeholders, interviews with the top management of
PP and interviews with the technical and field staff of the project activity.
This verification report covers the period from October 20, 2021, to December 31 2022.
The objective/scope of the verification is to have an independent review ex post determination by a VVB
(Validation and Verification Body) of the monitored GHG removals by sink that have occurred because
of the implementation of the project activity during a defined monitoring period.
Method and Criteria: Verification was conducted using RINA procedures in line with the requirements
specified in the VCS Requirements, i.e. VCS Program Guide, VCS Version 4.2 of 22/06/2021, and VCS
Standard, Version 4.4 of 21/12/2022. The GHG emission reductions estimated are based on the
approved Baseline and monitoring methodology VM0007: REDD+ Methodology Framework (REDD+
MF) version 1.6 of 08/09/2020.
The RINA team confirms in this final report that the information contained and the emission reductions
and/or removals claimed in the Monitoring Report are calculated in compliance with the requirements of
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 2

INTRODUCTION
1.1 Objective
RINA Services S.p.A. (hereinafter referred to as RINA) has been commissioned by BRCarbon
Serviços Ambientais LTDA to perform an independent verification of its CCB & VCS REDD
project, “Brazilian Amazon APD Grouped Project”, in accordance with the VCS standard version
4.4 and CCB standard v 3.1.
The reported GHG emission reductions for the given monitoring period must undergo
independent third- party verification and certification of emission reductions as the basis for
issuance of Voluntary Emission Reductions (VERs/VCUs).
The objectives of this verification exercise are, by review of objective evidence, to establish that:
• The project activity has been implemented and operated as per the project description
(PD) and that all physical features (technology, project equipment, and monitoring and
metering equipment) of the project are in place.
• Monitoring report and other supporting documents are complete.
• The data is recorded and stored as per the monitoring methodology and approved
monitoring plan.
• To confirm that the monitoring system is implemented and fully functional to generate
Voluntary Emission Reductions (VERs/VCUs) without any double counting, and
• To establish that the data reported are accurate, complete, consistent, transparent, and
free of material error or omission by checking the monitoring records and the emissions
reduction calculation.
1.2 Scope and Criteria
The verification scope is:
• to verify that actual monitoring systems and procedures are in compliance with the
monitoring systems and procedures described in the monitoring plan.
• to evaluate the GHG emission reduction data and express a conclusion with a reasonable
level of assurance about whether the reported GHG emission reduction data is free from
material misstatement.
• to verify that reported GHG emission data is sufficiently supported by evidence.
• to verify that the project activity has been implemented in accordance with the validated
Project Description.
• assess whether the demonstration of intent before the start date was sufficient.
The project is assessed against the of VCS program guide v 4.3, VCS standard v4.4 and CCB
version 3.1 standard and all the GHG program requirements RINA has, based on the
recommendations in the latest version of VCS Validation and Verification Manual v 3.2, and
employed a rule-based approach (as criteria) in the verification, focusing on the identification of
significant reporting rules and the reliability of project monitoring.
The Verification is not meant to provide any consultancy towards the project participants. However,
stated requests for clarifications and/or corrective actions may have provided input for improvement
of the Monitoring Report.

The final verification report before being submitted to the client were subjected to an independent
internal technical review to confirm that all verification activities had been completed according to
the pertinent RINA’s instructions, with reasonable level of assurance.
In addition, RINA applies materiality threshold of 1 per cent with respect to omission or
misstatement concerning reported quantities as per VCS Standard for large project.
The technical review was performed by a technical reviewer(s) qualified in accordance with RINA’s
qualification scheme for VCS validation and verification.

2.2 Method and Criteria
The verification was conducted using RINA procedures and applying the approved methodology “REDD+
Methodology Framework (REDD+ MF) - VM0007” version 1.6 /10/ and its associated tools, also in
accordance with the requirements of the VCS Standard version 4.4 /06/ and CCB standard version 3.1 /07/
and standard auditing techniques.
RINA carefully verified each table in the emission reduction calculation spreadsheet for the project area
during the monitoring period from October 20, 2021 to December 31, 2022. Additionally, the carbon
calculations are thoroughly verified and cross-referenced and are fully consistent with the validated
values. The project area is extensively reviewed such as GIS databases and shape files ensuring 100%
validation process. To assess whether the PA contains ineligible areas, the project area was cross-
checked with the requirements of the methodology, the standard VCS, the information in the MR and the
observations from the field visit. To do this:
• The KML files of the project made available were analysed to confirm that they accurately
delimit the project zone, showing the boundary of each instance included in it.
• A risk-based review was carried out.
• It was checked whether the project area overlaps with Conservation Units, Indigenous Lands,
Settlements and Quilombola Areas.
Verification process consisted of the following three phases:
• Document Review, investigation on secondary sources of applicable information.
• Follow-up actions- presential audits, remote meetings, including interviews with project
stakeholders and site assessments using satellite imagery, GIS, shape files of PA, videos, and
pictures of the site.
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 10
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
This monitoring period for the Brazilian Amazon REDD+ Project involves the second verification of PAIs
1-5 and the inclusion of new instances, consisting of the validation of PAIs 06-09.
For the second verification of PAIs 1-5, document checking procedures, verification of shapes and
satellite images, and analysis of the ER spreadsheet were conducted, as no changes were implemented
that impacts the applicability of the methodology, additionality, or the appropriateness of the baseline
scenario, according to VCS Standard v4.4, Section 4.1.9. An on-site visit was made to PAIs 2-5 in the
period 05-April-2023 to 09-May-2023 (more details in section 2.5). Regarding PAI 1, a remote audit
was carried out, considering that this is the second verification of the monitoring period and that this
approach is in line with section 4.1.9, 4.1.10 and 4.1.26 of VCS Standard 4.4. It should also be noted
that, as verified in VCS MR /01/, this instance has no CCB activity implemented and of the PAIs already
validated, it corresponds to the one with the smallest project area and lowest estimated VCU value.
For the validation and verification of PAI 6-9, field visits were conducted where the respective
stakeholders were consulted (see Section 2.4), the boundaries of farms and project areas were checked
through land displacement and aerial overflights, and remeasurement of permanent plots launched by
the PP to estimate biomass.
To carry out on-site visits, this VVB considered a sampling plan based on the number of PAIs added in
this monitoring. To achieve this, the team of auditors defined that the conduct of onsite visits should
represent at least 90% of the VCUs intended by the PP in the new instances. In this way, PAIs
6,7,8,9,10,14,15 was visited, which correspond to approximately 93.4% of the VCUs of the new
instances added, meeting the criterion of representing at least 90% of the VCUs of the new instances.
The sampling approach adopted ensured that all identified stakeholders were consulted, while at the
same time ensuring a high representativeness of the VCUs estimated by the PAIs sampled.
However, during the process of validating and revising the project, the PP did not follow up on the
inclusion of some of the new sites, removing sites 9-14 (Fazenda D'Graus I, Fazenda D'Graus II,
Fazenda D'Graus VI, Fazenda Nova Esperança, Fazenda São Domingos and Fazenda Capinzal I) from
the project activity, reclassifying the former PAI #15 - Fazenda Ribeiro Rondon as PAI #9 - Fazenda
Ribeiro Rondon. Thus, considering the change, the sites that remained and were validated (PAIs 6-9)
correspond to the same ones that were visited on site, so all the new sites were sampled.
To audit the forest inventories, the VVB adopted a procedure based on random sampling, analysis of
statistical parameters and the expertise of the audit team. Initially, a basic sampling is established,
consisting of at least four plots randomly selected by phytophysiognomy. The random selection of plots
by auditors is crucial to avoid bias when choosing the areas to be audited.
During the execution of the audit, the measurements obtained are analyzed in comparison with the
information reported by the PP. This comparison is carried out considering the values of each individual
presented in the PP's inventory, and also considering the calculation of descriptive statistics of the
inventory, such as coefficient of variation, confidence interval and error limit. These last two are
particularly useful, as the confidence interval indicates the range of values within which we can
reasonably expect the true population mean to lie. This is essential to determine whether
measurements are within an acceptable margin of error in relation to the reported data.
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 11
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
If the results obtained during the measurement of the plots are very close to the values reported by the
PP, or within the expected confidence interval, we can infer that the initial sampling was sufficient to
adequately represent the forest population, considering that the plots were selected in a truly random
manner, which minimizes bias and increases the reliability of inventory results.
However, if there are significant variations beyond what was expected or if the measurements are
outside the confidence interval, it is necessary to expand the sample to include more plots. This aims
to increase the representativeness of the inventory and reduce the uncertainty associated with the
results.
Additionally, if trends are identified in the audited portions, such as systematic underestimation or
overestimation, the audit team requests the PP to review the field procedures and calculation
methodologies. This ensures that any errors are corrected, and that the final data is robust and reliable.
During the audit process, project participants provided 100% of the data used in the calculations for
determining the final reported greenhouse gas reductions. The raw data were acquired from reliable
sources and are comprehensively documented within the project materials, which have been provided
to the verification team.
RINA carried out on-site inspections in the project area and carefully compared the collected data and
reproduced the calculations. RINA therefore confirms the accuracy of the data in the monitoring reports
and can demonstrate net reductions in anthropogenic greenhouse gases (GHGs), supported by
verifiable and reliable evidence.
2.3 Document Review
The CCB & VCS Monitoring report /01/, and additional background documents related to the project
monitoring (i.e. VCS Monitoring Report Template, Approved VCS methodology, Verification
Requirements) as well as scientific literature and country law were reviewed in accordance with VCS
Standard v4.4 /06/ and CCB Climate, Community and Biodiversity Standards v3.1 rules /07/. The
Document Review included an examination of the project details, data and parameters, and
quantification of GHG reductions.

2.4 Interviews
Interviews were conducted in April and May 2023, during on-site inspections and remotely, with the internal
communities (P.A Bom Jesus, T.I Barreirinha, P.A Águia, P.A Rio das Cruzes, P.A Diamantina, Vila Brasil,
Vila Curara, Porangaba and Palmares), project proponents, external stakeholders, people from local
environmental institutions, farm employees and governance representatives. For interviews, the different
stakeholder groups identified in the MR /01/ were selected according to their role in the project, their
influence on the development of the project at a local or regional level, the activities already carried out and
their location in the project area. In the interviews, the impact of the project was assessed: on changes in
well-being, on collective and/or conflicting rights, on current or past land disputes, relocation of human
settlements due to the project and how the project's activities considered the social, economic, and cultural
diversity within the local stakeholder groups.
The interviews confirmed that the stakeholders are aware of the project, its benefits, and impacts. They
also described and confirmed the activities already carried out and those planned and reported having a
communication channel with the BRC. However, during the interactions it was found that the project has
opportunities to improve in terms of following up feedback from the different stakeholders, with active
communication and an ombudsman.

2.5 Site Inspections
The site inspection of the Project Area was carried out from 10/04/2023-15/04/2023 in the state of
Pará, from 20/04/2023-25/04/2023 in the state of Amazonas and from 04/05/2023-09/05/2023 in the state
of Acre. The purpose of the on-site visit was to understand out and evaluate whether the project area
corresponds to the Project Description, Monitoring Report, and geoprocessing data (GIS). Also, to confirm
that the project complies with the eligibility requirements and rules of the VCS, the applicability conditions
of the methodology, as well as confirming the validity of information presented in the non-permanence risk
report and application of quality control and assurance procedures. During the on-site visit, the areas of the
properties Lagoa Grande II (PAI06), Nubia Floresta (PAI07), Floresta 1 (PAI08) and Ribeiro Rondon
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 52
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
(PAI09) were visited, and their boundaries were confirmed in the field using the Geo tracker app /86/. The
sampling method used by the PP to launch the plots was based on the establishment of fixed area transects,
each transect is made up of 8 plots measuring 20 x 60m. For this reason, the transects visited were
measured in their entirety, that is, the 8 plots of the transect were measured. In total, four transects were
visited and measured, each consisting of 8 plots, totaling 32 plots visited. The table below indicates which
transects were visited in each new PAI, as well as the number of plots measured and the coordinates of
some of them.

2.6 Resolution of Findings
The objective of this phase of the verification was to resolve any outstanding issues that needed be
clarified prior to RINA’s positive conclusion on the monitoring report.
A corrective action request (CAR) is issued if one of the following occurs:
• The project participants have made mistakes that will influence the ability of the project
activity to achieve real, measurable additional emission reductions.
• The VCS requirements have not been met.
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 54
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
• There is a risk that emission reductions cannot be monitored or calculated.
A clarification request (CL) is raised if information is insufficient or not clear enough to determine whether
the applicable requirements of VCS Version 4.4 standard and CCB standard 3.1 have been met.
The No. of CARs/CRs raised by RINA are the following: 07 CRs and 01 CAR
2.6.1 Forward Action Requests
A forward action request (FAR) is raised during verification to highlight issues related to project
implementation that require review concerning monitoring and reporting during the next monitoring period
and subsequent verification of the project activity.
A forward action request (FAR) should be issued, where:
1. the actual project description/ provisions for project monitoring and reporting practices requires
attention and /or adjustment for the next consecutive verification period, or
2. an adjustment of the MP is recommended.
The No. of FARs raised by RINA are the following:
2 FAR. The FAR is listed in Appendix 2 of this report.

3 VALIDATION FINDINGS
3.1 Participation under Other GHG Programs
Not applicable. The project is not registered nor seeking registration in any other GHG program, rather than
VCS and CCB.
3.2 Methodology Deviations
The PP proposes the following methodology deviations:
1) CAB_non-tree,i,t-carbon stock in aboveground biomass in non-trees is considered as part of the
average aboveground biomass stock subjected to burning (Bi,t) in VMD0013 v1.2, equation 2 in
the baseline and project scenarios.
2) An initial participatory rural appraisal (PRA) of the communities inside and surrounding the project
area was performed to determine if there was the potential to occur illegal logging activity.
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 55
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
Considering GPD initial scope, it remained characterized that all families living inside the project
area explore the forest for timber and fuel wood, what shall be considered as a low impact activity
driven by subsistence purposes. No timber or fuel wood economic activity was identified by BRC
field team. In this sense, BRC will assume that extraction of trees for timber or fuelwood is a
constant in PAIs where there are communities inside. Rather than making a PRA in every two
years, BRC will monitor the forest degraded area annually considering the protocols established in
this monitoring plan. This constitutes a methodology deviation which leads to more accurate and
conservative estimates since significance of timber and fuel wood extraction GHG related
emissions will always be tested.
3) Monitoring period: The first monitoring period extends from August 2nd (project start date) to
October 19th, 2021 (first monitoring report closing date). BRC proposes a methodology deviation
related to the minimum duration of a monitoring period, to be applicable only in the first monitoring
event.
4) During this monitoring period, a methodological deviation was also applied to one of the Vegetation
Suppression Authorizations (ASV) used in the proxy-based approach to define the D%. The
deviation in question is in relation to item 6 of section 5.1.3 of module VM0006 /16/, which states
that deforestation in the proxy area must have occurred within the 10 years prior to the baseline
period. However, for one of the properties used in the proxy area approach, the vegetation
suppression authorization submitted was issued in 2022 and executed in 2023, which is later than
the first year of the project baseline.
The deviation applied does not affect the accuracy and conservatism of the data, since it maintains
the use of an official document authorized by government institutions for the analysis of the proxy
area, which accurately portrays the dynamics of the project's proxy area in order to define the
deforestation rate. Furthermore, all the other criteria for the applicability of proxy areas for
determining the rate of deforestation are met in this proxy area in question.
RINA agrees that these methodological deviations will not adversely affect the conservative nature of
quantifying GHG emission reductions and ensure an equivalent level of accuracy in GHG estimates.
3.3 Project Description Deviations (Rules 3.5.7 – 3.5.10)
Three deviations were applied to the Project Description. The first refers to the above-ground carbon
estimates have been revised through the implementation of a new and improved allometric equation. This
updated equation, as presented in Chave et al., 2014 /88/, offers advantages compared to the previous
one, it incorporates two additional parameters, beyond tree diameter, which are an environmental
coefficient for estimating tree height and the wood density of the tree.
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 56
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
During the audit process, the PP was questioned about the reasons for requesting this deviation from
the methodology, mainly regarding the equation selection aspects of the Chaves et al. (2014) /88/ and on
the use of LiDAR data.
It was explained by PP that between the preparation of MR 1 and MR 2, BRCarbon's technical team
underwent changes with the addition of new engineers and technicians.
Among the new technical reinforcements were specialists in LiDAR technology, namely the current
director of REDD projects, Mr. Danilo Almeida, who brought the use of LiDAR to the company's projects as
an innovation capable of increasing the accuracy of estimates and increasing the quality of projections.
Through LiDAR surveys it was possible to obtain the altimetric structure of the areas flown over, and
increase the sampling area of the PAI's, not restricting the input data only to the areas of the field plots.
After collecting the LiDAR data, the PP also started to adopt the equation by Chaves et al. 2014 /88/
in place of Nogueira et al. 2008 /102/, and the reasons given were as follows:
• The equation proposed by Chaves is used in similar works that involve the use of LiDAR
data.
• The equation proposed by Chaves et al (2014) is widely used and cited, with more than
2000 scientific citations, corroborating its scientific applicability.
Another argument presented by the PP was regarding the increased accuracy of projections resulting
from the greater representability of the data from Chaves et al. To prove this, a comparison was presented
between the data used by Chave et al (2014) and Nogueira et al (2008), where the data from Chave et al
(2014) bring 182 trees with DBH > 80 cm, while the data from Nogueira et al (2008) has only 5 trees with
DBH > 80 cm.
Finally, a comparison of the estimates using Chaves et al and Nogueira et al was presented, where
both presented a difference of less than 5%, not representing a large difference that impacts the
acceptability of the Chaves equation.
It is worth noting that the PP presented the methodology used to estimate the various parameters, the
field inventory was carried out, which was verified during the onsite visit, where measurements of Diameter
at Breast Height (DBH) were repeated for some of the individuals sampled on the transects, which were
identified with numbered plates /28/. The project also carried out the UAV-LiDAR survey, which was
confirmed by reviewing the documentation provided, shapefiles, forest biomass inventory, carbon maps
and LIDAR estimates for PAIs# 02-05 and 07-09 /28/.
The adjustment in the equation does not impact the applicability of the methodology, nor does it affect
the additionality of the project, or the baseline scenario adopted. Even though there is a subtle increase in
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 57
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
the potential for VCU (Verified Carbon Units) generation due to the adoption of a more precisely allometric
equation, this does not interfere with the baseline scenario adopted by the project or with the hypothetical
conservation scenario without carbon project activities. Although, this deviation complies with section 3.20.2
of VCS standard 4.4 and CCB Standard version 3.1 /06/ /07/, where the PP has described and justified the
reasons for the deviation in a revised version of the project description /02/ /28/. It was confirmed that the
steps for estimating the above-ground carbon stock were followed, according to part 1, option 1 of the VCS
Module VMD0001 /12/ and other parts of the procedures of this same module for estimating carbon stocks
in the above- and below-ground biomass in live tree and non-tree pools, and the applicability of the
allometric equation used was also demonstrated /03/.
In addition, PP carried out an analysis of the Chave’s equation /118/, showing that the Chave equation
has the same or better predictive capacity than the Nogueira equation. It was also shown that the allometric
model was selected in accordance with section 6.1 of VMD0015 v.2.2 /19/, of "(e) Specific pantropical forest
type" and, likewise, the criteria "(a) Specific national species, genera and families", where the environmental
factor (E) of the Chave’s equation controls for a natural difference between countries within the tropical
region of the globe.
In addition to evaluating the model, the audit team also reviewed the application of the LiDAR model
for carbon estimation. The LiDAR application methodology was in accordance with VALBUENA, R. et al
(2017) and considered acceptable. For error calculation in these estimates, each 35x35 pixel of the carbon
maps was treated as a sampling unit. Adhering to the methodology suggested by Verra /10/, the carbon
average and confidence intervals of the estimates were calculated. This involved conducting inventory
simulations in a resampling process with 1000 iterations, extracting samples with replacements in each
iteration. The respective average estimates and the uncertainty of tCO²e per hectare were computed in line
with the Verra Methodology and aligned with the IPCC standards, maintaining a 90% confidence level. The
culmination of this process involved summarizing the 1000 inventories from the iterations to derive the final
confidence interval and carbon estimate, with the final uncertainty of the average carbon stock for all Phyto
physiognomies /28/ is less than 10% of the average value.
Based on the comprehensive review and analysis carried out, it can be confirmed that the
methodologies and practices employed in this carbon project are solid, robust and in line with the required
standards, thus providing a reliable and accurate assessment of carbon stocks.
RINA agrees that this deviation from the Project Description increases the accuracy of the carbon
calculation estimates and is therefore acceptable, since a more accurate and representative model was
used for the various forest landscapes of the project, due to the broader scope and with the inclusion of
additional environmental and density parameters, and the inclusion of more individuals. The deviation is
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 58
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
adequately described and justified, and the project remains in compliance with the rules of CCB Version 3
and the VCS /06/ /07/.
The second project description deviation is related to the inclusion of the project in the Gold Level
Criteria for Exceptional Biodiversity Benefits. The biodiversity monitoring has shown that six species found
in the project area are classified by the IUCN Red List as being at high risk of global extinction and therefore
meet the vulnerability criteria set out in the Climate, Community and Biodiversity Standards: v3.1 (GL3.1.
a) /07/.
The third deviation from the PD was related to the inclusion of new entities. To validate this, the
identification of the implementing partners in the CCB&VCS MR /01/ was verified, as well as their roles and
responsibilities in relation to the project, including, among others, project implementation, management,
and monitoring, during the period of obtaining project credits, in accordance with the rules of CCB Version
3 and VCS Standard 4.4 /06/ /07/. The information on the implementing partners presented in section 2.1.4
of the CCB&VCS MR /01/ has been cross-checked with the ownership documents /97/ /98/ and the
partnership agreement with the project proponent /24/.
In this deviation, the partner implementing PAIs #1-5 changed from BATISTA E CIA LTDA to Yaco2
Gestão e Comércio de Carbono. As presented in section 2.2.4 of the CCB&VCS MR /01/ and confirmed in
the ownership and partnership documents /97/ /25/, the two companies belong to the same owner, with
only the specification of Yaco2 Gestão e Comércio de Carbono to operate in the carbon market, but with
the management of the businesses of the owner of the two companies as the only liberality. Regarding the
new entities as implementation partners during the MP, they were added due to the inclusion of new
instances of project activities, PAI#06-09. Considering that this is a grouped project, and that the inclusion
of the new instances followed the scalability limits for grouped projects in the validated PD /02/, the addition
of new implementation partners is inherent in the project's growth structure. It should be noted that the
inclusion of the new instances was carried out in accordance with sections 3.6.16 and 3.6.17 of VCS
standard 4.4 /06/ (see section 3.5 of this report), as well as checking the documentation of the partners'
properties that relate them to the project areas and the partnership agreement established with the PP /97/
/25/.
Thus, the Allometric Equation, the Biodiversity Gold Level and the Inclusion of New Entities, have been
appropriately described and justified in section 2.2.4 of the CCB&VCS MR /01/ as a deviation from the PD,
in accordance with the rules of CCB Version 3 and the VCS /06/ /07/.

The audit team validated the minor changes by considering Rules 3.5.6 of the CCB Program Rules v

4.1 Public Comments (Rules 4.6)

4.2 Summary of Project Benefits
The summary of project benefits has been adequately set out in section 1 of the grouped project MR
/01/, establishing the activities to be implemented and their respective impacts on the project.
Benefits include actions to prevent and fight forest fires, digital inclusion actions for traditional
populations, conservation of forest massifs and preservation of local biodiversity, including endangered
species.
The mentioned benefits seem adequate to the reality of the place where the new PAI's are inserted.
Although most of the benefits cited have not yet been monitored or implemented, they are properly
characterized as future or ongoing actions.
The climate benefit is that the project aims to help mitigate climate change by conserving 27,626.34
ha. RINA confirms that the project complies with all the requirements of the Verified Carbon Standard and
Climate, Community & Biodiversity Standards, having estimated 2,458,294 tCO2 equivalents net emission
reductions during the crediting period of 20-October-2021 to 31-December-2022.
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 64
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
• The benefits for the local community and other stakeholders focus on the social impacts of the
project, with the development of activities in education, health, access to water, employment, and
training. The aim is to influence social issues and living conditions in the communities surrounding
the Project Area, reducing social vulnerability, and increasing the level of socio-economic
conditions and the quality of life of families, helping them to obtain goods and services that promote
economic activity and social well-being. To achieve these impacts, towers were installed to provide
internet, optical fiber, solar panels, and a battery bank. Training, improvements to the structure of
schools, access to water and well-being were also carried out. These activities are detailed in
section 4.3 of the MR /01/.
• For biodiversity, the benefits are the maintenance and monitoring of forest cover in the Project Area
and the reduction of GHG emissions from forest fires, guaranteeing the protection and conservation
of habitats and local biodiversity, including species with some degree of threat according to the
IUCN /87/.
• The VVB verified the results reported by the project for the benefits achieved for the climate,
community and biodiversity and confirms that during verification BRCarbon has the experience and
team to carry out activities during the life of the project.
4.3 General
4.3.1 Implementation Status (G1.9)
The project implementation status is explained in detail in section 2.2 of the MR /01/. As per the
interviews had with the PP, onsite visit and the supporting evidence provided to the verification team, it is
confirmed that the project is fully operational, and all the monitoring activities as discussed in the registered
PD have been completed. Thus, there are no material discrepancies between the project implementation
and the project description.
In this monitoring, a deviation was applied to the project description, where the above-ground carbon
estimates were updated by changing the allometric equation used /88/, so the ex-ante Baseline Emissions
were updated. Another deviation related to the inclusion of the project in the Gold Level Criteria for
Exceptional Biodiversity Benefits was also applied. Such deviations did not lead to any material
discrepancies between the implementation of the project and the project description.

4.3.2 Risks to the Community and Biodiversity Benefits (G1.10)
The steps taken to verify the natural and human-induced risks to the project's expected benefits
identified by the project proponent were:
- Assessment of the actions required and implemented to mitigate the likely natural and human-
induced risks to the expected climate, community, and biodiversity benefits during this monitoring period.
- For each identified risk, it was assessed whether the necessary measure had been outlined and
actions taken to mitigate.
- The risks identified in section 2.2.6 of the MR /01/ were cross-checked with supporting evidence, site
inspections and interviews.
The risks to the project presented by the proponent are valid for most of the preservation projects
developed in the Brazilian Amazon biome.
In general, they include risks of forest fires resulting from human actions, the project's inability to
prevent deforestation also resulting from third-party action, and deliberate sabotage for political reasons.
Predatory hunting is also presented as a risk to the project.
Among the natural factors, El-Niño is cited as a possible cause of prolonged droughts.
All actions that pose risks to the project will be attacked by the proponent, mainly through frequent
monitoring of areas and intervention actions, participation, and dialogue with local communities.
In general, the risks presented, and the mitigating actions are reasonable to the reality of the region
where the project areas are located. This was confirmed in section 2.2.6 of the MR, which was developed
in accordance with section G1.10 of PD /02/. The audit team assessed the main risks of the project and the

4.3.3 Community and Biodiversity Benefit Permanence (G1.11)
As presented in section 2.2.7 of MR /01/, the project landowners have signed a legal agreement /25/
to continue the management practices that lead to the conservation of the project area. This agreement
establishes the continuity of management practices that will lead to the conservation of the project area for
at least 30 years, to guarantee carbon stocks and benefits for the community and biodiversity. Such
measures aim to maintain and improve biodiversity, community, and climate benefits. They are therefore
reasonable and allow the project to continue beyond its lifetime.

4.3.6 Stakeholder Participation in Decision-making and Implementation (G3.6)
The participation of communities close to the project was evidenced during the on-site audit.
Community members referring to PAI 6 listened attentively to the proponents about what a carbon
project is and how they could benefit from it and were open to participating and supporting the project. As
reported by the community members, they feel forgotten by the government, which hasn't visited them for
over 3 years. The only exception is the Secretariat of Indigenous Health, which visits the villages once a
month. However, they lack all kinds of material resources and structures, such as electricity and
communication, which are still precarious in the area.
The leaders shared their needs with the company, listing them in order of priority. In addition to
supporting local schools, the most awaited help is related to obtaining drinking water and basic sanitation
for the communities, which was transmitted to the proponent as the first and main demand of the Curara
community, close to PAI 6.
With the verification of the activities implemented during the audit interviews and according to what is
presented in section 2.3.10 of the MR /01/ of the Stakeholder Participation in Decision-making and
implementation process, it was found that the PP enabled the participation of all communities who want
and need to be involved in the implementation, monitoring, and evaluation of the project. It also confirmed
the use of BRCarbon's Social Engagement Protocol /35/ /114/ to facilitate stakeholder participation in
decision-making.
The stakeholders interviewed during the site visit gave a positive response about the project and in
some cases suggestions for improvements, but in general, showed great interest in the continuity of the
project activities.

4.3.8 Stakeholder Feedback and Grievance Redress Procedure (G3.8)
The PP has developed a PP Grievance Feedback and Redress Operating Procedure /02/ /63/ which
outlines a clear grievance redress mechanism. The procedure was assessed by the VVB during the site
visit and found to be adequate for resolving complaints.
Regarding PAI 6, the feedback received from the Curara community was positive, where they showed
excitement and hope with the project, since they feel abandoned by the public power and all help is
welcome, according to their leaders. In relation to CASAI (Indigenous Health Care Center) they were also
favorable and excited about the possible partnerships that will be signed with the project.
Manicoré City Hall was open to working with project representatives on behalf of communities and
municipalities. However, among city hall representatives, a certain distrust was demonstrated regarding the
achievement of objectives and partnerships since they had already had negative experiences with other
supposed project developers. He was also questioned about the ownership of the farm, asking if the
ownership documentation had been presented, which was confirmed and indicated in which book and sheet
of the local registry office the documents presented are. Ownership of the Manicoré farm was also
confirmed with the land ownership documentation provided /97-100/ and the land analysis process carried
out /93/.
Finally, an agreement was reached between representatives of the bidder and the city hall, where it
was established that in the next visits by the bidder to the project area, 2 members of the city hall will be
invited to accompany them, since the place is difficult to access and the City Hall claims it does not have
the resources and staff to serve the communities in that region.
Regarding the other PAIs, there were also positive feedbacks regarding the implemented activities and the
interest in continuity, the stakeholders were very hopeful and engaged with the project. During the audit
visit, some community members from PAI#7-9 pointed out some questions regarding the quality of the
water from the well-established procedure. As a form of redress, BRCarbon sent a sample of water from
Vila Brasil's artesian well for laboratory analysis, where the result showed that the water quality is within
the chemical average allowed by Brazilian National Legislation. The PP also established a procedure to
check whether flavored water is related to the amount of iron contained in it (see Appendix 2: item 5 - CR
05).
About PAI#1-5, during the onsite visit, some residents were dissatisfied with the land regularization
activity. The BRC clarified that participation in the project activity would only be for those who wished to
take part. Those who accepted the offer had their area demarcated and georeferenced /68/. Public
