4.3.9 Worker Relations (G3.9 – G3.12)
It was verified by the audit team that the PP is committed to comply with all Brazilian laws and
regulations applicable to workers' rights, in addition, they have informed all workers about their rights, prior
to hiring, as described in the Brazilian Labor Laws /90/. PP ensures continuous interaction with the local
community. All members of the local communities have the same opportunity to attend training workshops
and participate in different activities, regardless of gender, race, religion, or sexual orientation. It was
explained that employees are selected based on their qualifications, skills, and experience. Community
members were given job opportunities without any discrimination based on gender, race, religion, sexual
orientation, age, physical or mental disability or social class.
A risk analysis for worker safety was also carried out by the PP. Daily activities have been proposed, such
as the Daily Dialogue on Safety, Environment and Health (DDSMS), which will be promoted to inform
workers about their rights and about their obligations as individuals and as a team. Trainings on disease
and accident prevention are foreseen for workers linked to the project, along with clarifications and general
education and carry out actions that minimize accidents and damage to workers' health. Appropriate care
for emergency situations will be proposed. Actions aimed at minimizing risks to workers' health will involve
promoting conditions to preserve the health and safety of all workers.
The audit team checked all documents included in MR sections 2.3.15 and 2.3.16 regarding the
occupational safety assessment /25/ /34/ /37/ /38/ to confirm that all positions and situations that could
provide some kind of occupational hazard have been avoided and mitigated. It is worth noting that, in
accordance with section 2.3.13 of MR /01/ unlike the other PAIs, during this period of agreement no training
was carried out among PAI#7-9, however, for the next monitoring period this is the case, and such activities
will be included in the report.

4.3.13 Legal Status (G5.6)
The MR /01/ listed all the national and local laws and regulations in the host country that are relevant
to the project's activities. As presented, the project complies with the Federal Constitution, the National
Environmental Policy, the National Land Planning Policy, the Vegetation Code, the National Climate
Change Policy, State Law 2.308 of October 2010, which established the State System for Incentives for
Environmental Services - SISA and the Incentive Program for Environmental Services - ISA Carbono, Law
no. 1.904 of June 5, 2007, etc. The project is in line with the National Policy on Payment for Environmental
Services (PNPSA) and applied the approach of free, prior, and informed consent (FPIC) /31/, in accordance
with ILO Convention no. 169 and the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples.
All PAI activities were implemented in compliance with current legal requirements. The audit team
verified, through a review of relevant national and local laws, interviews, and an on-site visit, that the project
complies with applicable national and local laws and regulations.
For the validation of the new instances included in the "Brazilian Amazon APD Grouped Project", RINA
had the support of legal consultants who analyzed the land documents provided by the project proponent
for "Proof of title" analysis. A report /99//100/ has been issued by the legal consultancy comprising the
analysis of titles (and relevant chain of titles) and the Proponent's legitimate ownership of properties in the
municipalities of Rondon do Pará, Tomé-Açu, Ipixuna do Pará and São Domingo do Capim in the State of
Pará and Manicoré in Amazonas state, corresponding to the new PAIs included in the "Brazilian Amazon
APD Grouped Project". For this purpose, the quality of the title and ownership were examined, as well as
the existence of propter rem obligations (obligations inherent to the property). Contingent liabilities that
could restrict or hinder the development of carbon credit projects were also analyzed, with risk being
understood as any event that could turn it into a real loss, resulting in the project holders not having a clear
and valid legal right to transfer the carbon credits.
Based on applicable Law, doctrine, precedents and experience, it was classified each identified risk
as follows: (i) Probable contingent liability or obligation - highly likely to occur before the Project is
implemented or throughout its development; (ii) Possible contingent liability or obligation - more-likely-than-
not chance of being realized, but are not necessarily considered unlikely either, including while the Project
is in development; and (iii) Remote contingent liability - not likely to occur at this moment and throughout
the development of the Project.

In view of the Scope, the documents provided by the Client and/or Owners, which were made available
until October 2023 /97/ in a virtual Data Room, were reviewed; according to the list of required
documentation /97/ /98/ /99/.
BRAZILIAN AMAZON APD GROUPED PROJECT is including new instances to the project’s activities,
related 4 (four) properties owned by the following individuals and organizations, all of them considered as
“Proponents” of the Project: 1 – Gilson Antônio Moreira Machado; 2 – Dione da Silva Machado; 3 – Brasil
Indústria e Comércio de Madeira Ltda; 4 – Adão Ribeiro Soares; and 5 - Incorporadora Manicoré Ltda
(“Owners”).
The Owners are the owners of the following Properties, comprising the areas included of BRAZILIAN
AMAZON APD GROUPED PROJECT, divided into 4 (four) properties:
1- Fazenda Floresta, with an area of 1,452.1373 hectares, enrolled under n. 5.564 at the Real Estate
Registry Office of São Domingos do Capim and Ipixuna, State of Pará; 2- Fazenda Floresta I, with an area
of 1,392.4409 hectares, enrolled under n. 7.419 at the Real Estate Registry Office of São Domingos do
Capim and Ipixuna, State of Pará; 3- Fazenda Rondon, with an area of 5,082.0000 hectares, enrolled under
n. 2.752 at the Real Estate Registry Office of Rondon do Pará, State of Pará, and 4- Fazenda Lagoa Grande
II, with an area of 39,622.8300 hectares, enrolled under n. 2.178 at the Real Estate Registry Office of
Manicoré, State of Amazonas (together “Properties”).
The following aspects were analyzed:
1- Update Real Estate Records indicating liens, debt, or attachments and or any other encumbrances
relating to the Property(ies) regarding the 20 (twenty) past years. ((Law 10,406/2002 - Brazilian Civil Code;
Law 6,015/1973 - Law of Public Records)
2- Certificate issued by the relevant Real Estate Registry Office with the complete chain of domain its
transfer from Public Domain to Private. ((Federal Constitution/1988 - Brazilian Federal Constitution; Law
10,406/2002 - Brazilian Civil Code; Law 6,015/1973 - Law of Public Records.)
3-CCIR (Rural Property Registry) before INCRA (Law 4,504/1964 - Rural Land Statute) - Except for the
CCIR referring to Fazenda São Domingos, which according to the Proponents will be issued after
regularization and unblocking of the real estate record that is in progress as above, VVB was provided the
current CCIR of the Properties.

5- ITR Debt Clearance Certificate. (Law 9,393/1996 - Law of ITR - Rural Property Tax) – VVB received the

CONCLUSION
As per the analysis of the Due Diligence Documents Owners hold regular titles to the Properties. As of the
date of this analysis (November 2023), in view of the Proof of Title Documents, it was not identified (i) any
legal actions questioning the legal transfer of Properties from the public domain to the private ones, (ii) third
parties’ claims related to the Owners' rightful possession of the Properties, that could hinder the legitimate
development of the Project.

4.4 Climate
4.4.1 Accuracy of GHG Emission Reduction and Removal Calculations
According to the monitoring report /01/ the data and parameters were reviewed and verified, according
to the methodology applied /10/, in relation to the validated PD /02/ and the monitoring plan.
The PP submitted emission reduction calculations and other supporting calculations in excel sheets. The
excel sheets are clear, un-protected and easily viewable. The calculation in the ER spreadsheet /03/ is
verified and found be correct. The methods and formulas set out in the project description for calculating
baseline emissions, project emissions and leakage are correctly followed in the Monitoring Report, version
4.0 of 13/11/2023 and version 4.2 of 06/05/2024 /01/ and ER calculation sheet /03/. All the values are
provided in the MR and ER calculation sheet are cross verified with its sources and confirmed no manual
transposition errors between data sets have occurred. Also, the consistency of values within MR, version
4.0 of 13/11/2023 and version 4.2 of 06/05/2024 /01/ is checked and found to be satisfactory. Hence
verification team conclude that the GHG emission reductions and removals have been quantified correctly
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 85
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
in accordance with the project description and applied methodology. Where applicable, references for
analysis methods or standard values have been checked against the relevant scientific literature for best
practice.
To quantify the current carbon stocks in the project area, the modules defined in methodology VM0007 /10/
were used. Baseline emissions were estimated according to module VMD0006 v1.3 /16/. The quantification
of baseline emissions was verified by analyzing deforestation, changes in carbon stocks, non-CO2
emissions and net GHG emissions in the baseline scenario. The project's GHG emissions are estimated
according to module VMD0015 v2.2 /19/. The verification team assessed the emissions reduction
calculation spreadsheet /03/, GIS data, remote sensing images and land use changes /29/ /38/ /39/ /40/
/41/.
The baseline considers that the most likely scenario is that the planned deforestation will be preceded using
fire as a tool to facilitate forest clearing. The zero values assigned to post-deforestation carbon stock in
deadwood (CDW,post,i) and litter (CLI,post,i) are based on evidence /115//116/.
To confirm the legal permissibility of the deforestation area allowed for the new PAIs, the compliance of the
areas with the requirements of the Brazilian Forest Code (LAW No. 12,651, OF MAY 25, 2012) was
assessed. Thus, considering the provisions of the Brazilian Forest Code, rural properties in the Amazon
biome need to preserve 80% of the forest cover, which guarantees rural property owners the right to remove
up to 20% of the forest cover on their properties for productive purposes. As confirmed by the analysis of
the maps and shapefiles of the project area /28/ /38/ /39/ /40/ /41/, the remaining forest cover on the rural
property in the spatial analyses supports the delimitation of the project area in each PAI in accordance with
the validated PD /02/. It was also found that steps are taken to review and redefine previously established
boundaries, using high-resolution images. In this way, legal permissibility is considered to be the area of
deforestation allowed in relation to the total area of the property, including areas that have already been
deforested. In order to assess whether the project area of each new PAI is a suitable area for conversion
to alternative non-forest land use, the accessibility of each PAI to the relevant markets was checked, based
on confirmation of livestock data and PRODES deforestation data /39/ /41/ /111/.
The accuracy of the PRODES classification used by the project was verified by cross-checking the GIS
evidence for each PAI with the methodology /119/ and images from the Amazon Deforestation Monitoring
Program (PRODES). VVB carried out a visual interpretation analysis of the 200 points randomly distributed
over the reference region.
To assess the accuracy of the PRODES classification, a comparison was also made of each class on the
land cover map for the reference period (2011 to 2021 for Acre and 2012 to 2022 for Pará). The VVB
verified that the classification interpreted by the PP was correct and agrees with the results presented. The
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 86
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
accuracy of the classification was 95.5%. Therefore, VVB concludes that the data used by PP to estimate
the area of deforestation recorded is accurate and reliable. These combined approaches helped provide a
comprehensive assessment of the accuracy of the PRODES-estimated deforestation data used by the
project.
Based on the maps presented, the suitability of soils, topography and climate was verified, which can also
be corroborated by the states' own ecological and economic zoning. As presented in section 3.2 of the MR,
it was confirmed that proximity to urban centers and meatpacking plants makes beef cattle production
possible, while medium and large properties make calf production possible. Thus, it is duly demonstrated
that the suitability of the project area for conversion to alternative non-forest land use can be proven by the
presence of similar economic activities in the surrounding area.
With regard to the deforestation area (Aplanned,i), according to section 2.2.4 of MR /01/ it has been
defined in line with VMD006 v1.4, in which the intention to deforest was demonstrated by proving the
existence of an immediate and site-specific threat of deforestation /113/. Evidence of intent to deforest was
presented as follows: a valid and verifiable land use management plan for deforestation in the project area.
It should be noted that the intention to deforest for the new PAIs included was demonstrated in accordance
with the validated PD /02/.
For PAI 6, the intention to deforest was demonstrated by the document Request for Approval_Ex-
Owner, dated 08-October-2021 /52/, which granted the former owner authorization to suppress vegetation.
The Request is dated prior to the signing of the conservation agreement with the project proponent.. In
PAI#7-8, which have the same owner, the intention to deforest is demonstrated by the request for
government approval/53/ /54/. They are dated before the instance start date. And for PAI#09, the intention
to deforest was demonstrated by document report 15498 (2), dated 18-August-2020, which is also
corroborated by the document “Espelho Supressão - Faz Ribeiro”, dated 18-August-2020, which was
issued by the government department responsible in the state and shows the history of the process for
obtaining the deforestation authorization. The document proving the intention to deforest predates the
signing of the conservation agreement with the project proponent /55/. Thus, the applications submitted are
dated before the project start date, confirming that the areas qualify for the application of VMD0006. These
applications were also used to demonstrate the intention to deforest as required by Section 5.1.2 of the
module.
Thus, for each new PAI included, the origin of the evidence presented and its relationship with the
specified instance was verified, as well as its sufficiency to demonstrate the intention to deforest. It was
confirmed that the deforestation dates of the new instances predate the long-term conservation agreement
signed between BRC and the landowners, which corresponds to the start date of the project. Finally,
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 87
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
according to the evidence verified, it is assessed that it has been sufficiently demonstrated that all the new
PAIs face an immediate threat of site-specific deforestation.
Regarding defining the rate at which deforestation is projected to occur (D%), the proxy areas
approach was applied for PAIs 6 to 9. During verification, an analysis was made of the 6 proxy areas
selected by the project to assess the consistency of the information presented. The vegetation surplus of
the selected properties was validated by consulting the Rural Environmental Registry System (Sicar) and
the Land Management System (Sigef) /120/. For each of the areas, the polygons of the properties, legal
reserve areas and permanent preservation areas (APP) were downloaded /121/ to confirm the value used
as legally convertible.
To check the deforestation values and identify the years in which they occurred, the maps of
converted areas available in the PRODES/Deter system were obtained /119/. This analysis made it possible
to confirm that the areas of deforestation were duly within the spatial limits established by the vegetation
suppression permits issued /123/.
In addition, the soil, vegetation, climate, elevation, and slope maps of the areas in question were
reviewed and verified /122/, with the aim of ensuring that all the information met the technical criteria
established by the VMD006 tool. This additional check reinforced the project's compliance with the
requirements necessary for the application of the tool /16/. Finally, it was found that the formula provided
by the VMD006 tool was applied correctly by the project, resulting in an accurate determination of the D%.

Forest benchmark maps of the reference region, project area and leakage belt were derived from GIS
files and remote sensing imagery prepared by the project developer /01/. RINA validated and verified these
maps by comparison with the imagery they were digitized from. These maps were used to derive the
parameters related to the quantity of land use and land cover change observed.
Parameters COMFi, Gg, GWPg, FLU, FMG and FI were taken from the IPCC good practice guidance.
RINA team cross checked the documents /58/ to ensure the correct values had been applied.
The PP submitted emission reduction calculations and other supporting calculations in excel sheets.
The excel sheets are clear, un-protected and easily viewable. The calculation in the excel sheet is verified
and found be correct. The methods and formulae set out in the project description for calculating baseline
emissions, project emissions and leakage are correctly followed in the monitoring report and ER calculation
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 100
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
sheet. All the values are provided in the MR and ER calculation sheet are cross verified with its sources
and confirmed no manual transposition errors between data sets have occurred. Also, the consistency of
values within MR is checked and found to be satisfactory. Hence verification team conclude that the GHG
emission reductions and removals have been quantified correctly in accordance with the project description
and applied methodology.
Concerning the accuracy of the spreadsheet, formulas, and conversions, as well as the consistent use of
data and parameters, the PP has developed a comprehensive procedure to ensure data accuracy and
appropriateness. During the verification process, RINA observed that the PP conducted a rigorous QC/QA
(Quality Control/Quality Assurance) procedure for field measurements and assessed uncertainty. RINA
concludes that the Project Proponent has adhered to good practices in this assessment, and GHG
reductions have been accurately quantified in alignment with the project description and applied
methodology.
4.4.2 Quality of Evidence to Determine GHG Emission Reductions and Removals
To identify the evidence used to determine GHG emission reductions and removals, during the audit
the monitoring procedures for remote sensing/GIS analysis, QC/QA procedures, how the data is archived
and the frequency of monitoring/recording of the parameters used were verified. As stated in section 3.1.3
of CCB&VCS MR /01/, for all PAIs, the monitoring of project activities is established in accordance with
criteria VM0007 v1.6 section 9.3.1 and VMD0015 v2.2 /19/.
As confirmed in the on-site inspection and interviews (see section 2.4 and 2.5 of this report), BRC is
responsible for implementing the project activity, monitoring it, and digitally archiving the database. To this
end, the MR /01/ also shows its organizational structure, responsibilities and competencies of the personnel
who carry out the monitoring activities. For this monitoring period, it was noted that for each activity carried
out, such as Legal Protection, Surveillance, Biomass Inventory, FSC Certification, Leakage Mitigation and
Wildfire Prevention and Fire Fighting, the monitoring implemented was presented, as well as the methods
used to measure, record, store, aggregate, compare, and report monitored data and parameters.
During the audit process, the audit team checked the QA/QC process which were explained and
showed by the PP. The LiDAR data specialist was asked about the process for estimating biomass in the
different PAIs, used to quantify emissions, reductions, and removals during the project.
Field plots were launched in all PAIs. A total of 60 parcels were allocated in PAIs 1, 65 in PAIs #2-5,
62 in PAI#6 and 128 in PAIs #7-9.
During the field visits, plots were selected in different PAI'S for visit and measurement, with all
measurements observed in the field being consistent with the results presented in the field records,
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 101
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
including in relation to the identification and numbering of individuals (photos of the audit measurements
are available, if necessary).
In addition to field plots, some PAI used LiDAR data to increase the accuracy of estimates.
For PAI 02-05: For PAI 02 to 05, models were used integrating the height data obtained by LiDAR. A
comparison was presented between the projections using LiDAR data or just applying the equation Chaves
et al (2014) /82/, where the estimates for the two phytophysiognomies were 354 tCO2e/ha (Ab) 406
tCO2e/ha (Ab) without using data Deal, and 370 tCO2e/ha (Aa) and 402t tCO2e/ha (Ab) using LiDAR data.
It is worth noting that the Ab phytophysiognomy is more representative in the project, representing more
than 70% of the area, and it presented lower biomass values in the LiDAR surveys, which was considered
conservative by the technical team at VVB.
For PAI #6, a LiDAR flight was not carried out, due to the complexity of accessing the site (13 hours
by boat on the Madeira and Mataurá rivers). For this PAI, the Chaves et al. equation was used, applied with
field inventory data. Eight plots chosen by the VVB team were remeasured in this instance. The values
found are close to other PAIs and close to literature values used in similar REDD+ projects in Brazil.
For PAIs from 7 to 9, estimates were also made using an allometric equation with a syntax similar to
that proposed by Longo et al. (2016) who used LiDAR data to apply the technique called Top-Canopy-
Height” - TCH, used to estimate the average height of trees in a given area based on the heights of trees
collected with the LiDAR flight. The PP also presented a comparative table where the carbon stock
estimated with LiDAR data is lower than the carbon estimated only with the Chaves et al. equation, which
seemed conservative to the VVB evaluation team.
To verify and validate the statistical adherence of the new allometric model proposed by PP, three
model structures were evaluated and validated, including a non-linear model (similar to the one used by
Longo et al. 2016). Using summarized data of dry biomass from field plots with living trees, both thin (AVF)
and thick (AVG), the average of the canopy height model (“Top-Canopy-Height” - TCH) was employed as
an input variable. The model evaluation occurred in two stages. Initially, performance was measured by the
coefficient of determination (r²) and the root mean square error (RMSE). The chosen model achieved an r²
of 0.75 and an RMSE of 0.3 Kg/m² (or 3 tons per hectare). Subsequently, the generalization capability was
validated through the leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV) procedure. The relationship between the
observed and predicted values (obs ~ pred) by LOOCV was validated, testing the correspondence with the
null hypothesis of regression, which has a 1:1 ratio, where the intercept and angular coefficient are
statistically equal to 0 and 1, respectively. All of these details, along with additional information, are available
in Appendix IV of the document titled 'Appendix_VII_PAI_7-15_PA_Carbon_Pools_Measures.docx'. The
methodologies and findings demonstrate the robustness of the model, enabling it to accurately assess
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 102
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
above-ground carbon stocks on a comprehensive scale. This significantly refines the estimates that were
previously limited to field inventory data. Additionally, LiDAR estimates tend to be more conservative, often
lower than those from inventory assessments, attributable to the system's enhanced capability to detect
smaller-scale vegetation within the forest landscape. Therefore, the use of the LiDAR model was
considered appropriate/conservative and has been accepted by the VVB.
The evidence provided to determine the GHG removals reported in the Monitoring Report included
values, notations, units, and sources; the evidence was cross-checked with the GHG removals calculation
spreadsheet /03/. The GHG removals for the project reporting period are based on published studies,
remote sensing and GIS data, carbon stock measurements and calculation procedures and factors that
have been assessed by the verification team. The procedure for recording, transferring and final
transposition of data was also verified, which is in line with the monitoring plan. It is therefore confirmed
through cross-checks that the appropriate monitoring mechanism is in place where the required parameters
need to be monitored.
The verification team has attained a reasonable level of assurance that these measurements and
procedures, including the internal quality control measures such as best practices in remote sensing and
GIS. All this reliable data, which is collected and documented, is used as a technical support tool for
decision-making in order to improve project outcomes, and to adapt the project according to the current
needs and realities.
The monitoring plan outlines mechanisms for internal data review and quality control, and the data
presented by the PP incorporates the outcomes of these internal assessments. RINA deems the information
supplied to be adequate, and the quality of this information is deemed suitable for assessing GHG
reductions in accordance with the Monitoring Report /01/, and the applied methodology.
The data and parameters used to determine GHG emission reductions are listed in sections 5.1, 5.2
and 5.3 of the PD/MR. As mentioned in the PD, all of the required data and parameters relevant to the
validation as of the date of this report and verification are available and provided in section 5 of the PD. All
the monitoring tools and procedures specified in the monitoring plan are applicable and eligible for VCS
requirements and the requirements of the methodology.
Interviews with project proponents and the examination of data and results have established that the
project proponents possess all the necessary competencies for accurately reporting GHG emissions
reductions. The data presented to the audit team was transparent, clear, and coherent, with processing
steps easily traceable to the corresponding sections of the methodology and monitoring plan, ensuring
transparency in the reporting process.
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 103

CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
Thus, the project duly demonstrates the reliability of the evidence and its source for determining
reductions or removals of GHG emissions. The flow of information from data generation and aggregation
to recording, calculation and final transposition into the monitoring report is also presented. RINA considers
that the evidence is sufficient to reproduce the calculations in quantity and quality and guarantee the
materiality established for the project.

4.5.1 Community Impacts (CM2.1)
Sections 4.1 and 4.2 of MR /01/ identify communities based on impacts and in general these impacts
are beneficial. The communities included in the assessment of welfare impacts have been identified as
applicable for this monitoring period and they are:
PAI#1-5: Porongaba, Palmares, Potiguar and Katiana.

The impacts, type of benefit/cost/risk, and change in welfare were clearly described in this section of
the MR /01/. All the listed communities benefited from the implementation of projects, such as internet
installation and purchase of equipment (TV, computer, and printer), installation of solar panels and energy
kits, financial and logistical support for actions aimed at community health, distribution of water filters,
training in sustainable livestock and pasture management, installation of an artesian well in one of the
communities, and activities with the education department to improve the structure of the school, among
other impacts.
BRCarbon bears all costs associated with the development, implementation, monitoring, and project
certification. No costs were passed on to traditional communities. The verification team took the following
steps to verify the reported impacts of project activities on the identified community group:
The VVB has reviewed Sections 4.1 and 4.2 of the MR /01/ and confirmed that it has assessed the
expected impacts on the well-being of communities, including all socio-economic or cultural groups, in the
with and without project scenarios.
The indicators, impacts, and changes in well-being are clearly described in the MR, which allows for
assessing the risks and benefits of the project for the community.
The VVB confirmed that the project uses adequate, foreseen, and real methodologies, costs, and risks
for each of the identified community groups.
The VVB interviewed local community members, who confirmed that the assumptions of the
community impacts model were discussed and explained to them and are a result of the ongoing
consultation process. The MR includes a detailed analysis of anticipated impacts, including costs, risks,
and benefits to communities, as well as evidence of support for these activities /31/ /32/ /33/ /34/ /35/.

partnership with the Sena Madureira Health Department has made it possible to take the "Itinerant Health"
program to more distant places that were not previously served. Given the difficulty in measuring the
benefits that occurred only due to the project, a letter written by the municipal secretary of Sena Madureira
/67/ acknowledging the BRC's help in collecting the area covered by the program was presented, reaching
the Porongaba Center where it was not scheduled to be part of the activity.

4.5.2 Negative Community Impact Mitigation (CM2.2)
Positive benefits of the with-project scenario for community groups, rural workers and local
communities were presented.
Section 4.1.2 of the MR /01/ describes some measures that were taken to avoid negative impacts on
the well-being of local communities and to maintain a good relationship with communities in the project
zone, such as placing signs in the areas of school to mitigate the impact that was caused by Internet access
in schools in Porongaba. Other measures are envisaged to improve the quality of health care, using
partnerships and advocacy as strategies.
However, the land regularization action, which includes the recognition of the right of possession, presented
to mitigate a negative impact, may be generating the opposite effect. During the field visit, the consulted
interested parties were more dissatisfied than satisfied with the supposed benefit, arguing that they would
have to change their production habits, as they would no longer be able to "drill" new areas to plant. This
negative impact, according to the interviewees, is more important than the positive impacts, such as the
provision of internet for local schools.
On this point, the PP clarified the stages involved in the land regulation process, as well as how this
delineation is being carried out. The BRC stressed that the land activity was only being carried out with
those who had agreed to take part, so that those who did not wish to be regularized retained all rights to
the land where they live and maintain their customs /68/. To mitigate the negative impacts, the PP presented
measures to strengthen new forms of production, using the rotating farm technique, which could improve

productivity and reduce the need to open new areas. Also described as mitigating activities already
implemented by the PP are the holding of public meetings with the communities, as well as maintaining
constant communication with the population, through field visits, telephone, and WhatsApp contacts,
keeping them informed about the process.
In any case, the mitigation actions have been implemented in accordance with the validated project
description. The status of the mitigation action, land-title regularization, will be monitored in the next
monitoring.

4.5.3 Net Positive Community Well-being (CM2.3)
It was assessed during verification that communities will continue with normal activities in the absence
of the Brazilian Amazon APD Grouped Project. The project was conceived, developed, and implemented
to address environmental and community issues in the project area. All benefits referred to in Section 4.5.1
of this report and Section 4.1.1 of MR/01/ are available.
The project has been shown to have a net impact on well-being compared to the “no project” land use
scenario.
According to MR /01/, the following social benefits were provided in the with scenario:
• Installation of photovoltaic systems, water pumps, and freezers in the houses;
• Donation of solar panel kits (inverters) and water pumps for families;
• Installation of 5 internet towers and more than 50 miles of fiber optics in 2021, and in 2022,
the installation of 4 more towers;
• Training in sustainable agriculture and livestock practices;
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 114

CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
• Workshops and training in sustainable livestock production for improve pasture management
and agricultural production;
• Increase in climate change adaptation;
• Improvements in water condition with the installation of an artesian well;
• Cooperation with public bodies to contribute to improving the infrastructure of PA Irmã Dorothy
and EMEF Urutum municipal schools.
RINA was able to verify the positive impacts for the communities served by the project through the
review of documents /33/ /34/ and interviews with members of different communities, as described in
Section 2.4. It was reported by stakeholders how the project activities benefited the communities and their
desire to remain. Therefore, the conclusion of the net impact of project activities on community groups is
positive.
4.5.4 Protection of High Conservation Values (CM2.4)
As described in MR /01/ section 4.1.4. and interview with stakeholders described above in section 2.4,
the High Conservation Value (HCV) "sites and resources fundamental to satisfy the necessities of local
communities or indigenous people: the Matuará River and Capim River" and HCV related to cultural sites
of local importance were not negatively affected during this monitoring period.
Also, according to the MR and interviews with the PP, the project will invest in actions related to forest
conservation and monitoring. The PP also commits to be in constant contact with the local population to
modify the project process whenever necessary, not only respecting their customs and cultures but also
reinforcing their social relations. The verification team interviewed community leaders or representatives to
confirm the impacts and did not evidence negative impacts on the HCVs identified during the second
monitoring period.
4.5.5 Other Stakeholder Impacts (CM3.2-CM3.3)
Negative impacts on other stakeholders are not expected, as described in section 4.2 of MR /01/ and
the same did not occur during this monitoring period. During the field visits, it was found that the project
activities are well received by the communities and already have positive impacts with training in livestock
and sustainable production, partnerships with community and public agencies for reform and construction
of schools, such activities extend to surrounding stakeholders. Thus, project activities do not negatively
impact the well-being of other stakeholders during the second monitoring period.

4.6 Biodiversity
4.6.1 Biodiversity Changes (B2.1)
Changes in biodiversity were verified based on what is presented in section 5.1.1 of MR /01/ and in
the monitoring reports developed for PAI#01, PAI#02-05, PAI#06 and PAI#07-9 /26/. Biodiversity has
changed in a positive way due to the maintenance of species richness and conservation of endemic and
endangered species. New Project Activity Instances, PAI#6-9, were included in this monitoring.
According to MR, the richness of species is achieved due to the maintenance of forest cover during
the monitoring period. In addition, fauna monitoring is recorded in the Biodiversity Monitoring Report 25/.
During the on-site visit, the areas where the forest cover is maintained were confirmed, where there are
signs identifying the area, to make it easier to locate the plots, there are also identification signs on the
individual trees and the presence of a camera trap used to monitor biodiversity was verified. The project
activities that justify these changes are:
• Long-term conservation agreement signed with the landowners /25/, which enables the
maintenance of forest cover.
• Initial description of biodiversity in the project area.
• Identification of priority species for conservation and study in the project area.
• Knowledge on forest and biodiversity conservation issues.
• Monitoring plan for four biodiversity groups (mammals, birds, reptiles, and amphibians),
considering four biodiversity variables (species richness, number of threatened species,
endemic species, and game species) /27/.
The VVB team verified that maintaining the current high level of biodiversity is appropriate and will
generate multiple co-benefits. The above parameters were also confirmed through on-site observations,
interviews with local experts and communities.
4.6.2 Mitigation Actions (B2.3)
No negative impacts on biodiversity were detected in the Project Area and Zone resulting from Project
activities.
The conservation of forest cover and maintenance of habitats promoted by the implementation of
project activities has reduced forest fragmentation, which has enabled the maintenance of species identified
as threatened and endemic (HCV).
The measures planned to mitigate negative impacts on biodiversity and for the maintenance or
enhancement of High Conservation Value attributes are described in MR /01/ as prevention of poaching,
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 118
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
vegetation and genetic diversity and buffer zone. Mitigation actions were verified during site visits,
interviews and with the review of biodiversity reference data /26/ /27/. The verification team confirmed that
the actions taken to mitigate negative impacts are in line with the project description.
4.6.3 Net Positive Biodiversity Impacts (B2.2)
The net impacts of the project on biodiversity in the project area and zone are positive compared to
the land use scenario in the absence of the project, where the common practice is deforestation for livestock
activities. The without-project scenario would lead to a drastic reduction of biodiversity over the years.
According to section 5.1.3 of the MR /01/ the implementation of the project activities enabled the
conservation of vegetation cover, habitat availability and species diversity since it avoided the clearing of
native forest. In addition, it enables the maintenance of species richness and conservation of endemic and
endangered species, as explained in section 5.1.1 of MR /01/. This has been confirmed from the review of
biodiversity data /26/ /27/ and through visits, interviews with experts and local communities.
The VVB's opinion is that the information contained in the MR is sufficient to meet the requirements of
this section, since the implemented plan has achieved the objective of net impacts on biodiversity that are
positive overall.
4.6.4 High Conservation Values Protected (B2.4)
In accordance with Section 5.1.4 of the MR /01/, the implementation of project activities resulted in the
maintenance of forest cover, which contributes to the conservation of habitats, species diversity, and the
protection of endangered and endemic species. The project zone is being conserved due to the activities
promoted by the project; therefore, the high conservation value area was not negatively affected by the
project. The information was checked through a review of biodiversity data /26/ and interviews with experts
and communities.
The VVB concluded that the project will not negatively affect any biodiversity-related HCVs.
4.6.5 Invasive Species (B2.5)
The project activities did not introduce invasive species of plant or animal of any kind during current
monitoring period. No invasive species were observed during the site visit, which was also confirmed by
reviewing the supporting documentation.
4.6.6 Impacts of Non-native Species (B2.6)
It was confirmed during the on-site visit that non-native species are not used in the project. However,
according to MR /01/ one species of non-native grass was introduced by the local inhabitants to raise cattle,
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 119

namely Brachiaria sp. Given the difficulty this species has in establishing itself in shaded areas, it was not
found to be present in the forested areas; this was verified during the on-site assessment and by checking
the monitoring records.
4.6.7 GMO Exclusion (B2.7)
Based on the onsite visit and interviews with communities, RINA found that GMO was never used or
proposed to be used during the monitoring period. This indicator is sufficiently addressed in the MR.
4.6.8 Inputs Justification (B2.8)
Through interviews and observations made on-site, the audit team confirmed the project team’s
assertion that no inputs such as any fertilizers, chemical pesticides, and biological control agents have been
used during the verification period.
4.6.9 Negative Offsite Biodiversity Impacts (B3.1) and Mitigation Actions (B3.2)
As per the implemented monitoring plan, there are no negative impacts on biodiversity which has a
positive effect even outside the project area. According to MR /01/, the activities promote forest cover and
consequently contribute to the conservation of habitats within and outside the project area and have started
to engage communities in the conservation of the environment. This was verified during the onsite visit and
interviews with communities.
As there are no negative impacts on biodiversity, no mitigation measure planned by the PP. This indicator
is sufficiently addressed by the PP in the MR.
4.6.10 Net Offsite Biodiversity Benefits (B3.3)
As per the implemented monitoring plan, there are no negative impacts on biodiversity which has a
positive effect even outside the project area. Biodiversity within the project zone is impacted positively
especially over the without project scenario. No negative offsite biodiversity impacts identified or reported.
This was verified during the onsite visit and interviews with communities.

collection and analysis process.
• The audit team analysed the IUCN list /87/ and confirmed the information presented on
species in the MR as Vulnerable, Threatened and Critically Endangered are occurring in the
region of the project area.
According to MR /01/, during this monitoring period the presence of three Endangered species (EN)
Inia geoffrensis (red river dolphin), Pteronura brasiliensis (giant otter) and Ateles chamek, and one Critically
Endangered species (CR) Psophia obscura were identified within the project area.. To confirm compliance
with the vulnerability criteria established in the Climate, Community and Biodiversity Standards: v3.1
(GL3.1.a), we checked: the fauna report corresponding to the PAIs, maps, traps where the camera traps
were installed /26/ and the supporting bibliography /27/.
For the species identified in the project according to the IUCN Red List /87/ as endangered or
vulnerable, the main threats described for them are habitat loss due to deforestation, degradation, land use
change and for some of the species hunting pressure and the pet trade were also described /27/ /91/ /92/
/93/ /94/. In this regard, the actions taken by project activities to maintain and improve the survival of trigger
species is to conserve forest cover and prevent illegal deforestation within the project area. According to
the PP, the project areas will function as remnants of biodiversity in a landscape that is mostly dominated
by pasture and agriculture.
During the site visit and interviews, the monitoring actions aimed at conserving the forest cover were
confirmed. Therefore, it can be concluded that the actions taken by the project can maintain or improve the
population status of each trigger species in the project area and reduce threats to them.
4.6.14 Optional Gold Level: Effectiveness of Threat Reduction Actions (GL3.4)
The steps taken to verify the measures taken to maintain or improve the population status of the trigger
species are the same as those described in section 4.61.3. According to section 5.4 of MR /01/, the

measures taken by project activities to maintain and improve the survival of trigger species consist of
conserving forest cover and preventing illegal deforestation in the project area.
In summary, it can be concluded that the actions taken by the project can maintain or improve the
population status of each trigger species in the project area and reduce threats to them.
4.7 Additional Project Implementation Information
Not applicable. No additional information on the project implementation is needed.

5 VERIFICATION CONCLUSION
RINA has performed verification of the “Brazilian Amazon APD Grouped Project” and has verified that
the project is in compliance with the Verified Carbon Standard version 04 and VCS Validation and
Verification Manual v 3.2 and ISO 14064-3:2019.
The verification process was performed on the basis of all issues and criteria of VCS. The conclusions
of this report show that the project, as it was described in the project documentation, is in line with all criteria
applicable for the verification. The VVB confirms that the project has been implemented in accordance with
the project description.
The audit team concludes, with reasonable assurance, that the quantification of the net GHG removals,
during the verification period, is free of material misstatement and complies with the verification criteria.
RINA is able to issue a positive verification opinion for the 2,458,294 tCO2 as reported in the Monitoring
Report.

order the unblocking of registration (art. 8), as follows:
Joint Provision nº 10/2012-CJCI-CJRMB
Art. 5th. Upon receipt of the Request for Requalification, the registrar shall notify the State or Union,
through its respective autarchy or land agency, within a period of 60 (sixty) days, if it so wishes, to
present a statement on the request, gathering the evidence it deems appropriate.
Art. 6th. Whether or not the statement from the bodies referred to in art. 5, and after the established
period, the requalification procedure must be completed in a maximum of 30 days by the registrar.
Sole Paragraph - The requalification will be carried out from the annotation to the margin of the canceled
registration, rendering the cancellation previously carried out without effect, without the need to open
a new registration, being prohibited, however, to promote the unblocking of the registration determined
by Provision n.º 013 /2006/CJCI/TJE/PA.
Art. 8th. Once the requalification procedure has been concluded, the respective Real Estate Registry
Office must forward, ex officio, a full authenticated copy of the records to the competent Agrarian Court,

(i) The notice of fact n°01.2023.00020090-3: This is a report of a fact forwarded by IBAMA, due to the processing
of an administrative process based on the infraction notice number LPING3TG, with a brief description:
"presenting false information in the SISFLORA system when receiving and holding 5,822.9431 m³ of forest credits
in logs and others...".
It is important to note that this administrative process is ongoing and awaiting a decision on the administrative
defense.
In its administrative defense, among all the arguments put forward, it is necessary to inform you that the company
that was fined has 100% of its timber sold from duly licensed and approved management projects, having
attached to the case file all the relevant licenses that demonstrate the chain of legitimacy and legality of its forest
product.
This administrative process is regarding the use of SISFLORA system, and has not been finalized yet, it’s
fundamental to highlight that this process doesn’t have any kind of impact on the property ownership and/or on
the rights on carbon credits.
The juridic report has been shared on file BRASIL-MADEIRAS_RELATORIO-01-MPE-IPIXUNA-DO-PARA.pdf
(ii) The lawyer landowner had access to this notice of fact notice No. 000769-082/2022 sent by SEMAS-PA on
24th October of 2023 it is also an administrative process, which deals with "presenting false information in the
SISFLORA system". This process is still pending judgment, is in progress/instruction and awaiting a decision on
the administrative defense.
Within the scope of the administrative defense, among all the arguments put forward, we have the fact that the
company that was fined has 100% of its timber sold from duly licensed and approved management projects,
having attached to the case file all the relevant licenses that demonstrate the chain of legitimacy and legality of
its forest product.
The statement of facts reported here falls exclusively on the CNPJ of the subsidiary, exempting the CNPJ of the
parent company.
The factual report sent to the State Public Prosecutor's Office (MPE) deals with the assessment of the facts and
the possible commission of the crimes described, provided for in article 46 of Law 9.605/98 and 299 of the Penal
Code.
To date, no action has been taken by the MPE, such as the filing of any criminal action, or communication with
the company Brasil Ind. E Com. Madeiras LTDA.

CR ID 05 Section no. 4.1 Date: 30/06/2023
Description of CR
NET POSITIVE COMMUNITY IMPACTS
1. Although the project claims to have participated in the expansion of the "Itinerant Health" project, during
interviews with stakeholders, members of the health secretariat reported that the "Itinerant Health" project
already existed prior to the implementation of the project activity. Please, clarify the number of benefits and
beneficiaries of the health program described in Section 4.1.1 and provide evidence that these benefits did not
occur - or at least not to this extent - in the pre-project period.
2. During the field visits, it was identified that the PP promoted some cultural benefits in the Acre region (Porongaba
Community), such as help in the June cultural festivities, donation of materials to the church, such as Bibles,
rosaries, among others. On the visit to Amazonas, it was also verified that the PP has been supplying gas, diesel,
and other materials to the Curara village. These cultural and community support benefits are not identified in
the MR, so it is recommended to add these benefits.
3. In section 4.1.2, land regularization is presented as a mitigation to the negative impacts to the community and
is described: “…To mitigate these negative impacts on disputes, we have promoted a dialog with local
communities and the landowner aimed at agreeing on measures to address deforestation based on land
donations. Due to the bureaucratic challenges, this will take some time, which may cause frustration for people
waiting for the paperwork…”
However, during the field visit, the consulted stakeholders were more dissatisfied than satisfied with the
supposed benefit, arguing that they would have to change their production habits as they would no longer be
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 153
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
able to "drill" new areas to plant. This negative impact, according to the interviewees, is more important than the
positive impacts such as providing internet for local schools. The audit team requests a comprehensive list of
steps to obtain land tenure from a legal perspective. It is not clear whether delineating potential ownership
boundaries for each family is the only step in the legal process and how this will be done.
4. Please clarify what measures will be adopted to mitigate the negative impact related to land regularization.
5. During the field visit, community members presented some complaints about the water quality of the installed
artesian well, some reported possible allergies related from the water. PP is requested to present the measures
needed and taken to mitigate this negative impact.
Project participant response (PP 1st response) Date: 11/08/2023
1. Through the partnership of BRC and the Sena’s Madureira Health Municipal Secretary the Itinerant Health
Program achieved for the first time the people from Porongaba’s centre that is placed far from the riverbanks.
And because of that this area use to stay out of the program. In 2022 the activity happened in three different
areas – Nova Olinda, Recife and Porongaba, and 1665 attendances were made by doctors, dentists, nurseys,
and others, but unhappily it is not possible to measure properly the ones that happened just because of our
project. Nevertheless, it is evident that carbon project enabled and fostered some public actions for very distant
communities and, until then, little attended by public actors. To find the information updated, see section 4.1.1
Community Impacts at 2nd MR.
2. The demand was inserted on MR, but it is necessary to clarify that instead Curara village occurred some
donations in other villages around PAI#07-15. Other point to highlight is that the training activities, the field day
of rural extension, was realocated as an activity of Cauaxi REDD+ Grouped Project (AUD) which happens at the
same PP instances. To find the information updated, see section 4.1.1 Community Impacts at 2nd MR.
3. The Porongaba’s occupation reality is basic divided between two groups the one that is traditionally there, living
for a long time in Porongaba’s site, and the outsiders who has just arrived on the territory, and they are grabbing
lands and been the main deforestation vector in the propriety and in the project area. Seeing it as a threat to
the project the BRC together with the landowner designed a proposal to the good faith squatters about land
regularization, recognising their legitim right to the land. It was given to them an option to accept or not, who did
not want the regularization kept their whole right to the land where they live and keep their customs. The ones
which have accepted the offer had their area demarcated and georeferenced. The minimum size of the lots
which was defined as 30 ha, if they had less than that opened the regularized lot could have this size, and if
they had already opened a larger area before demarcation the regularized lot kept its size. After that, the owner,
and the responsible technician for the georeferencing process made a formal request to dismember the
registration: 5355 Fl 01 Lv 02(RG) CRI/Sena Madureira-AC at the National Institute of Agrarian Reform (INCRA),
this document will be attached for evaluation. Further than, was happened a public meeting on March 2023
when was showed for those interested, the steps of necessary bureaucratic process to finish the donation.
Through a flowchart the BRC team described step by step how the donation process flows, at this time the
process is at final step registering new enrolments at the registry office and determining the donation tax that
will be paid to issue the public deed of donation.
4. Although the proposal of land regularization was created to mitigate deforestation process, this activity has
generated negative community impacts as described and recognised at the section 4.1.2 of the MR. It is
important to highlight the project main target is the forest conservation, even though the traditional
communitarian “drilling” is a common practice and seeing as natural and necessary the BRC has never coerced
or prohibited the population to keep them. To address this issue pointed there will be taken measures as the
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 154
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
strengthen new forms of production, through rotative technic of swidden areas which can improve productivity
and reduce the need to open new areas. Further than that, after the lots of land’s dismemberment the settlers
who accessed this right will be rewarded annually with crediting in a proportion of the maintenance of forested
area inside the lots. The BRC’s team is concerned about this situation and is aware about the challenge to
change this cultural practice, and how long it might take.
5. A water sample from this artesian well in Vila Brasil was sent for laboratory analysis, the result of which shows
that the water quality is within the chemical average allowed by Brazilian National Legislation. Although, the
complaint about flavoured water is seen to be related with larger quantity of iron in it if compared with the water
they commonly use to consume. To try to solve this problem, the PP will follow three steps: 1) Check the water
tubes to see if the iron is coming out of there; 2) Check the water’s tank material quality to see if that is flavouring
the water; and 3) Do a residual chlorine analysis to see if is it needs to be added to the water for health reasons.
Documentation provided by project participant
1. Pictures and payment receipt
2. Document and donation pictures
3. Land regularization documents
4. Water analysis report
5. ---
VVB assessment Date: 22/08/2023
1. The PP claims that its partnership with the Health Department of Sena Madureira has made it possible to reach
the "Itinerant Health" program to more distant places, which were previously not served. However, the PP admits
the difficulty in measuring the benefits that occurred only due to the project. As evidence of the proponent's
participation in the aforementioned project activities, proof of payment in the amount of R$ 985 was presented
for the project's action in the Nova Olinda, Porongaba and Recife rubber plantations. The PP is requested to clarify,
please, how it is possible to state that the event only reached the population of the center of Porongaba (far to
the riverside) due to BRC funding, since as stated there is no way to adequately measure the impact of the
intervention. This item is still open.
2. The identified benefits were entered into the MR. Item closed.
3. The PP presented the procedure being adopted to address the issue of land regularization for squatters and
residents within the project area (Porongaba's site). Based on the evidence presented, the subject is being treated
individually for each case, with residents being offered the option of regularizing or not their properties. According
to the PP, the minimum area to be allocated to each family will be 30 hectares, which seems sufficient for the
development of agricultural activities (subsistence and small-scale commerce). The evidence and explanations
are sufficient to clarify the steps to obtain land ownership and how the potential property boundaries for each
family will be delineated. Item closed.
4. The PP presented its proposals to mitigate the impacts caused by land regularization, which include teaching new
forms of production, and pecuniary payments to landowners who maintain forest cover. However, no evidence of
informed procedures was presented. According to the requirements of the VERRA template and the CCB standard
v3.1 (CM2.2), it is necessary to describe the measures required and taken to mitigate any negative impacts on
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 155
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
the well-being of community groups and for the maintenance or enhancement of attributes of high conservation
value. Thus, the PP is asked to provide some evidence of procedures already reported. This item is still open.
5. The PP presented the analysis of water from the well in question, where the levels are within the limits allowed by
Brazilian legislation. To get around the iron taste of water, the proponent listed some measures that will be
adopted. This item was added to the open FAR to monitor the proposed actions. Item closed.
This CR remains opened.
Project participant response (PP 2nd response) Date: 01/09/2023
1. The evidence of our help to the Health Itinerant program of Sena Madureira is a letter wrote by the municipal
secretary recognizing the BRC’s help to raise the area covered by the program, achieving the Porongaba Center
where was not previewed to be part of the activity, that can be found on folder CR_ID_05 > “Declaracao_Pref-
Mun-Sena-Madureira.pdf”.
Other Health Itinerant program’s evidence is the Sena Madureira municipality official Social Media post,
announcing the occurrence of Health Itinerant activities on September 13th of 2022, that can be found on folder
CR_ID_05 > “Saude_itinerante_Pref-Mun-Sena-Madureira.pdf”.
4. The proposal of land regularization is an important strategy to achieve the main target of the project, avoid
deforestation. But, further than that, it is recommended, by the CCB, to recognize the tenure rights of traditional
populations, what can mean the donation of the traditional land used by families in the area. But to do it, it is
necessary to deal with some challenges which go from bureaucratic issues to new forms of production. In this
sense, it is possible to divide the question in different mapped issues:
a. Land Regularization - The slowness of the donation process
The service of georeferencing the lots was done, it counted 370 geodetic markers, referencing 40 land
plots. And the landowners who has accepted the donation were communicated about the bureaucratic issues,
more than that, in a public meeting occurred on March third, 2023, at Seringal Porongaba, the maps produced
by this georeferencing service was delivered to them to keep a transparent process. Beyond that, the project
proponents are in constant communication with population, maintaining them informed about the process,
which is slow, once some steps depend on public sector, what is out of PP´s control. All this communication is
done through field visits, phone, and WhatsApp contacts, but unfortunately the slowness of the dismemberment
and donation process, creates certain frustration to the people, what is understandable.
There was a delay in the process due to a halt in the SIGEF system in Acre state, which has now been
resolved. Currently the process is under SIGEF analysis, that is expected to be approved in the coming days.
After completing the SIGEF registry, the PP will proceed with the payment of fees and registration in the notary.
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 156
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3
Considering the current status and the next formal steps, the PP expects to have concluded all the 42 titles
within 40 days. To summarize, the workflow to conclude the property titles transference, encompasses:
- On-site demarcation.
- Recording the subdivision and the new properties in SIGEF.
- Payment of fees related to the donation.
- Registration with the notary based on the Property Description and Plan to issue the property titles.
To monitor the subdivision status, please access the SIGEF website: "Consulta" -> "Parcelas" and use Junior
Galvane's CPF (if necessary, we can provide the CPF number). The subdivision will be listed under "Seringal
Porongaba – Seringal Porongaba". We have attached a PDF (folder CR_ID_05 >
DesmembramentoProrongaba_Parcela-SIGEF_30ago2023.pdf”) of the page for easier viewing. In it, you can
find the links to the 42 plots plans and a note indicating automatic updates in the event of approval. More
related documents can be found Appendix VII.
b. Land Regularization – Avoiding forest area shrinking within the project boundaries
To avoid the forest shrinking inside the project area, some actions was taken, as the creation of a pecuniary
system payments to those who accepted the land regularization, which is yet under construction, to be well
attached between the law limits and the projects interest – avoiding deforestation. There is a draft been
developed to officially involve all the landowners. Through this document an agreement will be done between
PP and landowners to encourage them to maintain the forest cover in their areas, mitigating the deforestation
pressure.
See this document on Appendix VII.
c. Land Regularization - Income generation through sustainable production alternatives
The third strategy to avoid deforestation is fostering the smallholders to improve their production on the
area, changing the traditional practices of “drilling” to amplify productivity on a sustainable way, minding about
the maintenance of the forest instead of cutting it. In this sense, the project has been developing landowners’
productivity skills to relieve the pressure upon the forest, through training and qualifications, fostering
sustainable products chains and the development of one or two sustainable farm pilot project, that is currently
under construction together with the community. More details can be found on folder CR_ID_05 > “Diagnóstico
sócio produtivo dos sistemas agroalimentares do projeto GPD.docx”and CR_ID_05 on folder “Fotos sistemas
agroalimentares do projeto GPD”
CCB v3.0, VCS v3.4 157
CCB & VCS VERIFICATION REPORT:
CCB Version 3, VCS Version 3

included public meetings with the communities, as well as the bureaucratic stages of the process, an agreement to

dismemberment of the original farms has not yet taken place, the impacts and the procedures put in place to mitigate

4. Outros Elementos
Campos de preenchimento opcional
4.1 Informações sobre a performance da organização, em comparação com benchmarks internos (ex: outras
unidades)ouexternos(ex:organizaçõesdomesmosetor).
Nossas emissões diretas (escopo 1) de GEE totalizaram177.409,08 tCO2e no ano de 2023, o que significou um
aumento de 12% em comparação ao ano anterior. O principal fator de aumento foi a contabilização da operação
de Aripuanã por 12 meses, pois a operação foi iniciada no segundo semestre de 2022. Outra inclusão foi o
aprimoramentodacoletaecomissoocálculode“Processosindustriais”naunidadedeJuizdeFora.
Já nossas emissões referentes ao consumo e à compra de energia elétrica (escopo 2) totalizaram 5.481,82 tCO2e,
tambémrefletindoainclusãodeAripuanãnaapuração.
Vale destacar que a principal inclusão no inventário de 2023 foi realizada no Escopo 3. A partir de estudos
internos e benchmarks no setor da mineração e indústria do zinco, passamos a reportar as categorias mais
relevantesemvolumedeemissõesdoescopo3,quesãoascategorias1,10e11.Comisso,tivemosumaumento
importantenaquantidadedetCO2ereportada,quandocomparadaaanosanteriores.
Essa adequação do nosso inventário reflete a evolução e amadurecimento na gestão e no controle de nossas
emissões,alinhando-noscompadrõesglobaisdosetordemineraçãoe
preconizadospelaInternationalZincAssociation(IZA).
4.2DescriçãodeindicadoresdeemissãodeGEEparaasatividadesdaorganização.Porexemplo,tCO2e/produtos
fabricados.
A intensidade de emissões foi de 0,44 tCO2e por tonelada de zinco e óxido de zinco vendida. A intensidade é
calculada pela soma dos escopos 1 e 2 (escolha de compra) GRI 305-4. Este indicador posiciona a Nexa como um
dosprodutoresdezincocommenoremissãodeCO2domercadodestemetal.Mostrandoqueestamosalinhados
a nossa ambição de sermos um dos produtores de zinco com as menores emissões de gases de efeito estufa do
mundo.

Programa Brasileiro GHG Protocol
Esta Declaração de Verificação1 documenta que o Organismo de Verificação (OV)
citado abaixo realizou as atividades de verificação de acordo com as Especificações de
Verificação do Programa Brasileiro GHG Protocol e a norma ABNT NBR ISO 14064-3:2007.
Todos os campos são de preenchimento obrigatório.
Organismo de Verificação (OV) Organização Inventariante (OI)
Nome do OV: Bureau Veritas Nome da OI: Nexa Resources
Nome do verificador líder: Rubens da Silva Nome do responsável pelo inventário: Júlia
Ferreira Morelli Faria
E-mail: ferreira.rubens@uol.com.br E-mail: julia.faria@nexaresources.com
As emissões de gases de efeito estufa (GEE) informadas pela Organização Inventariante
em seu inventário de emissões, de 1º de janeiro até 31 de dezembro de 2023, são
verificáveis e cumprem os requisitos do Programa Brasileiro GHG Protocol, detalhados
nas Especificações do Programa Brasileiro GHG Protocol de Contabilização, Quantificação
e Publicação de Inventários Corporativos de Emissões de Gases de Efeito Estufa (EPB).
Nível de Confiança
O Organismo de Verificação (OV) atribuiu o seguinte nível de confiança ao processo de
verificação:
Verificação com nível de confiança razoável
“O inventário de gases de efeito estufa da organização inventariante para o ano de
2023 está materialmente correto, é uma representação justa dos dados e informações
de GEE e foi elaborado de acordo com as EPB.”
As limitações do processo de verificação foram: N.A.
Verificação com nível de confiança limitado
“Não há indícios de que o inventário de gases de efeito estufa da organização
inventariante para o ano de [ano] não esteja materialmente correto, não seja uma
representação justa dos dados e informações de GEE e não tenha sido preparado de
acordo com as EPB.”
As limitações do processo de verificação foram:
Inventário não verificável
Incluir razão, por exemplo: “devido a erros de dados” ou “não está de acordo com as
EPB”:

A Nexa é uma mineradora global e uma das empresas investidas da Votorantim ,

retornos financeiros superiores aliados a impactos sociais e ambientais positivos ,
as empresas de seu portfólio operam em 16 países em diversos setores. A equipe
da Nexa busca a excelência e acredita no que faz. Queremos dar um passo além,
pensando na qualidade e utilizando a tecnologia para inovar, criando um futuro
melhorparatodosnós.

Em 2024, fizemos pela primeira vez a Análise de Ciclo de Vida (ACV) de nossos principais produtos, com apoio e
orientaçãodeumaconsultoriaespecializadaeapósconclusãodaanálise,passamospelaetapadeverificaçãopor
terceira parte conforme as normas ISO 14071, 14040 e 14044. O estudo está sendo uma excelente fonte de
benchmarking interno e definição de estratégias comerciais. Um outro indicador que já acompanhávamos nos
anos anteriores é a intensidade de emissões. Em 2024 foi de 0,38 tCO2e por tonelada de zinco e óxido de zinco
vendida.Essenúmeroécalculadopelasomadosescopos1e2(escolhadecompra)GRI305-4,dividopelovolume
de zinco metálico e óxido de zinco vendidos. Este indicador posiciona a Nexa como um dos produtores de zinco
com menor emissão de CO2 do mercado deste metal, se usarmos a métrica que produtos com < 1t CO2/ t Zn
seriam classificados como um Zinco Low Carbon. Essa referência não foi definida pelo IZA, o International Zinc
Association,masinformalmenteutilizadaporváriasindústrias.OficialmenteoIZAindicouumamédiadaindústria
3,8tCO2/tZn,mostrandoqueestamosalinhadosanossaambiçãodesermosumdosprodutoresdezincocomas
menoresemissõesdegasesdeefeitoestufadomundo.

Contamos com inovação e novas tecnologias para cumprir nossas metas públicos assumidas sobre o tema de
emissões.Sãoelas:
❖ Redução absoluta das emissões de escopo 1 em 20% (52 mil toneladas de CO2 equivalente), mantendo a matriz
energéticadaNexaquaseemsuatotalidadecompostaporfontesrenováveis.
❖Alcançaraneutralidadedeemissõesaté2040.
❖NetZeroaté2050.
Temosprojetosparapossibilitaroatingimentodenossasmetasestratégicassobreessetema,garantindomelhor
desempenho no que diz respeito a energias limpas e redução da emissão de gases de efeito estufa (GEE) nas
operaçõesenacadeiadevalordonegócio.AlgumasdasaçõesemandamentoemnossasunidadesdoBrasiledo
Peru:
•Bio-óleo
O projeto visa introduzir um novo combustível biogênico para utilização na fábrica de óxido de zinco na unidade
01/08/2025 17:45 11
Inventário 2024 - Nexa
de Três Marias, reduzindo 25 mil toneladas anuais de CO2e, além de otimizar a performance da operação e
reduzir custos. Em 2024, foram concluídas as obras de engenharia para a conversão de 12 fornos, o que
promoverá uma redução anual de 6,44 mil toneladas de CO2e a partir de 2025. Novos fornecedores do
biocombustível vêm sendo desenvolvidos, mirando um fornecimento contínuo em larga escala com qualidade. A
conversãodosdemais35fornosestãocontempladosnoportfóliodeinvestimentosdelongoprazo,comprevisão
deconclusãoaté2029.
•Biobriquetes
NaoperaçãodeJuizdeFora,ozincosecundárioérecuperadoapartirdarotaWaelz,umfornorotativoqueutiliza
coque de petróleo como combustível que, além de ter grande impacto nas emissões de GEE da operação, é o
principal custo da unidade. Os biobriquetes, em desenvolvimento nos últimos anos, visam à substituição parcial
docoqueutilizadocomofontetérmica,eseusestudosdeviabilidadetécnicaforamconcluídosem2024.Ostestes
industriais de 2023 mostraram potencial técnico para substituir 30% do coque utilizado. Em 2024, estudos de
mercado e análises econômicas para escalonamento comercial dos briquetes revelaram desafios no custo de
produção. Em 2025, serão exploradas rotas alternativas, incluindo uma nova formulação de um briquete
autorredutor, para otimizar o consumo de coque nas reações químicas do processo de recuperação de zinco. O
desenvolvimento dos briquetes almeja a redução de 25 mil toneladas anuais de CO2e, aliada à competitividade
econômicaparaadiversificaçãodamatrizenergéticaporfontesbiogênicas.
• Logística sustentável Devido ao crescente interesse dos clientes nas emissões associadas ao transporte dos
produtos, implementamos cálculos de emissões em nossas principais rotas logísticas. Enfrentamos um grande
desafio na redução dessas emissões, e nossos times de Logística e Inovação têm trabalhado ativamente para
encontrar alternativas. Entre as iniciativas adotadas, destacam-se o aumento do uso de empilhadeiras elétricas
nas operações, a implementação de frotas movidas a gás natural veicular (GNV), a utilização da tecnologia
HydraGENTM e a expansão do uso de rotas multimodais (ferrovia + rodovia). Além da substituição de parte da
frota para veículos movidos a GNV, estamos em fase de negociação com armadores para a emissão de
certificados Eco para o transporte marítimo, por meio do uso de combustíveis verdes, reduzindo emissões em
rotasestratégicas.
4.4 Informações sobre contratos com clientes e fornecedores que incluam cláusulas vinculadas à elaboração de
inventáriosdeGEEe/ouaoenviodeinformaçõesrelacionadas.
Um dos destaques de 2024, apoiado pela nossa Estratégia ESG, foi a obtenção de um financiamento de R$ 200
milhões com o Banco Nacional de Desenvolvimento Econômico e Social (BNDES), com prazo total de 8 anos e 2
anosdecarência,quevisaapoiarpráticasemESGepossuicontrapartidasquealavancamaatuaçãonotema.Essa
foi a primeira operação de crédito vinculada a ESG do BNDES no setor de mineração, parte do programa BNDES
Crédito ASG. Uma das contrapartidas é a obtenção do Selo Ouro no padrão internacional GHG Protocol, usado
por empresas para atestar a qualidade do inventário de emissões de gases de efeito estufa (GEE). Após
cumprirmos o ciclo de participação e publicarmos nosso inventário de emissões de escopos 1, 2 e 3, ano-base
2023, no Registro Público de Emissões no Brasil, obtivemos o Selo Ouro em 2024, mais uma etapa importante
para o alcance das nossas metas de longo prazo. Em outro movimento estratégico em 2024, decidimos acessar o
mercadodecapitalbrasileiro,permitindootimizaraestruturafinanceira,diversificarasfontesdefinanciamentoe
melhorar a posição de liquidez. Como parte dessa estratégia, emitimos nossa primeira debênture vinculada a
ESG, no valor de R$ 650 milhões, com prazo de 6 anos. Esse instrumento e suas condições estão conectados ao
nossocompromissodereduzirasemissõesdeGEE.

Plataforma ESG (ESG Data Management): Em 2024 avançamos com a implantação da plataforma de gestão ESG
Deep ESG. Parametrizamos a coleta de dados para realização do inventário Brasil direto na plataforma, seguindo
a metodologia GHG Protocol. Treinamos os pontos focais e automatizamos diversos dados. O que traz maior
abrangência e transparência dos dados. Para o inventário Peru, revisamos a metodologia, adequamos fatores de
emissão aos mais adequados à realidade peruana. Traduzimos a plataforma, para que a língua não seja uma
barreira, ou fator de erro e treinamos os pontos focais. A medida que dados reais foram sendo inseridos na
plataforma, correções e oportunidades de melhorias foram sendo ajustadas e trabalhados em conjunto com o
fornecedor. O inventário foi realizado ainda seguindo a planilha do GHG Protocol, como “gabarito” ao que
devemosesperarencontrarnaplataforma.
ApósessaetapaderevisõesaplataformaestaráaptaaserabaseúnicaeoficialdoinventárioNexaem2025.
